| **Human Resources** | Compare headcount, attrition, or hiring across departments. |
| **Finance** | Compare revenue, expense, and profit across business units. |
| **Quality Control** | Compare Six Sigma defect counts by category. |
| **Healthcare (Hospital Mgmt)** | Compare patient footfall by department. |
| **Retail Analytics** | Compare basket size across store locations. |

---

## 2.5 Choosing the Right Bar Chart — Decision Logic

The KTU 2024 OEMET611 syllabus provides this decision logic:

**Step 1:** Are you comparing **one variable across categories**?
- YES → **Simple Bar Chart** (Vertical or Horizontal).

**Step 2:** Are you comparing **two variables across categories**?
- YES → Is the focus on **totals**?
  - YES → **Stacked Bar Chart**.
  - NO (focus on individual values) → **Grouped/Clustered Bar Chart**.

**Step 3:** Are you comparing **proportions (% share)** across categories?
- YES → **100% Stacked Bar Chart**.

**Step 4:** Do you have **> 10 categories** with **long category names**?
- YES → **Horizontal Bar Chart** (readability).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Construction Algorithm (Step-by-Step Derivation)

Below is the **complete, exhaustive 10-step construction algorithm** for a Simple Bar Chart — the KTU 2024 reference procedure. Every step is explicitly derived so you can reproduce it in the exam.

---

### **Problem Setup (Given Data)**

A retail store records the monthly sales (in ₹ Lakhs) of five products for the month of March 2025:

| Product | Sales (₹ Lakhs) |
|---------|----------------:|
| P1 - Basmati Rice | 45 |
| P2 - Toor Dal | 30 |
| P3 - Sugar | 60 |
| P4 - Salt | 15 |
| P5 - Cooking Oil | 50 |

Construct a Simple Vertical Bar Chart following the KTU 2024 OEMET611 construction standard.

---

### **Step 1: Identify the Categorical Variable and the Numerical Variable**

$$\text{X-axis (Categorical)} = \{\text{P1, P2, P3, P4, P5}\}$$

$$\text{Y-axis (Numerical)} = \{\text{Sales in ₹ Lakhs}\}$$

**Reasoning:** Categories must be discrete and finite; the numerical values must be measured on a ratio scale (zero is meaningful here since sales cannot be negative).

---

### **Step 2: Determine the Maximum Value for Y-axis Scaling**

$$V_{\max} = \max(45, 30, 60, 15, 50) = 60$$

**Reasoning:** The Y-axis must comfortably accommodate the largest data point with some headroom for data labels above the bar.

---

### **Step 3: Compute the Optimal Tick Interval (6–8 Tick Rule)**

The KTU 2024 OEMET611 standard prescribes **6 to 8 ticks** for clean readability:

$$\Delta y = \frac{V_{\max}}{7} = \frac{60}{7} \approx 8.57$$

Round up to the next "nice" number (multiples of 1, 2, 5, 10 are standard):

$$\Delta y_{\text{final}} = 10$$

**Reasoning:** "Nice" tick intervals (1, 2, 5, 10) are easier to read than awkward fractions.

---

### **Step 4: Set the Y-axis Upper Bound**

$$Y_{\max} = \lceil V_{\max} / \Delta y \rceil \cdot \Delta y = \lceil 60/10 \rceil \cdot 10 = 6 \cdot 10 = 60$$

**Reasoning:** The upper bound is the smallest "nice" number ≥ $V_{\max}$, giving exactly 6 ticks (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60).

---

### **Step 5: Compute the Scale Factor (k) for Drawing**

Assuming we want the tallest bar (P3 = 60) to be drawn at a height of **6 cm** on paper:

$$k = \frac{\text{Drawing height of tallest bar}}{V_{\max}} = \frac{6 \text{ cm}}{60} = 0.1 \text{ cm per unit}$$

---

### **Step 6: Compute the Drawing Height of Each Bar**

$$B_i = k \cdot V_i$$

For each product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{P1}} &= 0.1 \times 45 = 4.5 \text{ cm} \\
B_{\text{P2}} &= 0.1 \times 30 = 3.0 \text{ cm} \\
B_{\text{P3}} &= 0.1 \times 60 = 6.0 \text{ cm} \\
B_{\text{P4}} &= 0.1 \times 15 = 1.5 \text{ cm} \\
B_{\text{P5}} &= 0.1 \times 50 = 5.0 \text{ cm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Step 7: Determine Bar Width and Inter-Bar Gap**

Using the KTU 50% rule, with a chosen bar width of $W_b = 1$ cm:

$$G = 0.5 \cdot W_b = 0.5 \text{ cm}$$

**Total horizontal space per bar** (bar + gap):

$$W_{\text{slot}} = W_b + G = 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ cm}$$

---

### **Step 8: Compute the Total Chart Width**

$$W_{\text{chart}} = n \cdot W_{\text{slot}} = 5 \times 1.5 = 7.5 \text{ cm}$$

Adding margin for Y-axis labels (~2 cm on the left):

$$W_{\text{total}} = 7.5 + 2.0 = 9.5 \text{ cm}$$

---

### **Step 9: Compute Descriptive Statistics for Reference Line Overlay**

Mean of sales:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{45 + 30 + 60 + 15 + 50}{5} = \frac{200}{5} = 40 \text{ (₹ Lakhs)}$$

A horizontal **reference line** can be drawn at $y = 40$ to quickly identify products above/below average.

**Products above average:** P1 (45), P3 (60), P5 (50) — **3 products**.
**Products below average:** P2 (30), P4 (15) — **2 products**.

---

### **Step 10: Finalize the Chart with Title, Labels, and Data Labels**

- **Title:** *"Monthly Sales of Five Products — March 2025"*
- **X-axis label:** *"Product"*
- **Y-axis label:** *"Sales (₹ in Lakhs)"*
- **Data labels:** Display the exact value on top of each bar.
- **Sort order (recommended):** Sort bars in **descending order** for better visual ranking: P3 (60) → P5 (50) → P1 (45) → P2 (30) → P4 (15).

---

## 3.2 Grouped Bar Chart Construction (Step-by-Step)

### **Problem Setup**

A company wants to compare the **Q1 and Q2 sales (₹ Lakhs)** of three products:

| Product | Q1 Sales | Q2 Sales |
|---------|---------:|---------:|
| X | 40 | 55 |
| Y | 60 | 50 |
| Z | 30 | 45 |

**Total bars = 3 categories × 2 sub-categories = 6 bars**.

### **Step 1: Determine Y-axis Range**

$$V_{\max} = \max(40, 55, 60, 50, 30, 45) = 60$$

Using the same 7-tick rule, $\Delta y = 60/7 \approx 8.57 \rightarrow$ round to **10**.

$$Y_{\max} = 60, \quad \text{Ticks} = \{0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60\}$$

### **Step 2: Set Bar Width and Inter-Group Gap**

For grouped bars, the convention is:

- **Sub-bar width** (one sub-bar within a group): $W_{sb} = 1$ cm
- **Gap between sub-bars within a group:** $G_{inner} = 0.2$ cm
- **Gap between groups:** $G_{group} = 1.0$ cm

**Per-group width:**

$$W_{group} = 2 \cdot W_{sb} + G_{inner} = 2(1.0) + 0.2 = 2.2 \text{ cm}$$

**Total chart width (3 groups + 2 inter-group gaps):**

$$W_{chart} = 3 \cdot W_{group} + 2 \cdot G_{group} = 3(2.2) + 2(1.0) = 6.6 + 2.0 = 8.6 \text{ cm}$$

### **Step 3: Calculate Bar Heights Using $k = 0.1$ cm/unit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{X,Q1} &= 0.1 \times 40 = 4.0 \text{ cm} \\
h_{X,Q2} &= 0.1 \times 55 = 5.5 \text{ cm} \\
h_{Y,Q1} &= 0.1 \times 60 = 6.0 \text{ cm} \\
h_{Y,Q2} &= 0.1 \times 50 = 5.0 \text{ cm} \\
h_{Z,Q1} &= 0.1 \times 30 = 3.0 \text{ cm} \\
h_{Z,Q2} &= 0.1 \times 45 = 4.5 \text{ cm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 4: Add Legend**

- **Dark Blue Bar** = Q1
- **Orange Bar** = Q2

---

## 3.3 100% Stacked Bar Chart Conversion (Step-by-Step)

### **Given Stacked Data**

| Region | Online Sales | Offline Sales | Total |
|--------|-------------:|--------------:|------:|
| North | 30 | 20 | 50 |
| South | 40 | 40 | 80 |
| East | 20 | 30 | 50 |

### **Step 1: Compute Percentage of Each Component Within Its Bar**

$$V_{ij}^{\%} = \frac{V_{ij}}{T_j} \times 100$$

For **North**:

$$V_{\text{Online, North}}^{\%} = \frac{30}{50} \times 100 = 60\%$$

$$V_{\text{Offline, North}}^{\%} = \frac{20}{50} \times 100 = 40\%$$

For **South**:

$$V_{\text{Online, South}}^{\%} = \frac{40}{80} \times 100 = 50\%$$

$$V_{\text{Offline, South}}^{\%} = \frac{40}{80} \times 100 = 50\%$$

For **East**:

$$V_{\text{Online, East}}^{\%} = \frac{20}{50} \times 100 = 40\%$$

$$V_{\text{Offline, East}}^{\%} = \frac{30}{50} \times 100 = 60\%$$

### **Step 2: Verify All Stacks Sum to 100%**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{North: } 60\% + 40\% &= 100\% \;\checkmark \\
\text{South: } 50\% + 50\% &= 100\% \;\checkmark \\
\text{East: } 40\% + 60\% &= 100\% \;\checkmark \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The denominator in a 100% stacked chart is the **column (bar) total**, not the grand total. This is the most common error KTU examiners penalize.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Chart Type | Use When | Avoid When | KTU Marks Weightage |
|------------|----------|------------|---------------------|
| Simple Vertical | Compare 1 variable across ≤ 10 categories | Long category names, > 15 categories | 3 marks |
| Horizontal | Long category names, ranking required | Time-series data | 3 marks |
| Grouped | Compare 2 sub-variables across categories | > 4 sub-groups (becomes cluttered) | 7 marks |
| Stacked | Show totals + parts simultaneously | Precise sub-comparison needed | 7 marks |
| 100% Stacked | Compare **proportions** across categories | Absolute values matter | 7 marks |
| Pareto | Identify the "vital few" causes (80/20) | Data is symmetric, no skewed distribution | 14 marks (full question) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bar Chart Family Tree — Conceptual Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete taxonomy of bar charts** as per the KTU 2024 OEMET611 Module 3 syllabus. This is a high-level **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing the relationships between chart types.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Bar Chart Family] --> B[Simple Bar Chart]
    A --> C[Horizontal Bar Chart]
    A --> D[Grouped Bar Chart]
    A --> E[Stacked Bar Chart]
    A --> F[Hundred Percent Stacked]

    B --> B1[One bar per category]
    B --> B2[Categorical X axis]
    B --> B3[Numerical Y axis]

    C --> C1[Categories on Y axis]
    C --> C2[Values on X axis]
    C --> C3[Best for ranking]

    D --> D1[Two to four sub groups]
    D --> D2[Side by side bars]
    D --> D3[Legend required]

    E --> E1[Bars stacked vertically]
    E --> E2[Shows total and parts]
    E --> E3[Sum equals grand total]

    F --> F1[Each bar equals 100 percent]
    F --> F2[Sub components as percent]
    F --> F3[Best for proportion comparison]

    A --> G[Pareto Chart Special Case]
    G --> G1[Descending sort]
    G --> G2[Cumulative percentage line]
    G --> G3[Follows 80 20 rule]
```

---

## 4.2 Bar Chart Construction Workflow — Process Flow

This Mermaid flowchart depicts the **complete 10-step construction process** for any bar chart, as derived in Section 3.1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify Categorical and Numerical Variables] --> S2[Step 2: Find Vmax from dataset]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Choose Tick Interval using six to eight rule]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Set Y axis Upper Bound to nice number]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute Scale Factor k equals drawing height by Vmax]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Calculate Bar Heights using Bi equals k times Vi]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Set Bar Width and Inter Bar Gap using 50 percent rule]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Compute Total Chart Width]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Compute Mean and Standard Deviation for reference line]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Add Title Labels Legend and Data Labels]
    S10 --> FIN[Final Validated Bar Chart]
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Correct Bar Chart Type

This diagram helps students **navigate from data type to chart selection**, a common KTU Part A question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What is your data?] --> Q2{Discrete categories?}
    Q2 -->|No| H1[Use Histogram or Line Chart]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Number of variables?}
    Q3 -->|One| SC[Simple Bar Chart]
    Q3 -->|Two or three| Q4{Focus on totals or values?}
    Q4 -->|Totals| STK[Stacked Bar Chart]
    Q4 -->|Values| GRP[Grouped Bar Chart]
    Q3 -->|Proportions| PC[100 Percent Stacked Bar Chart]

    SC --> Q5{Category names long?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| HZ[Use Horizontal Bar Chart]
    Q5 -->|No| VC[Use Vertical Bar Chart]

    STK --> PR{Cumulative effect needed?}
    PR -->|Yes| PAR[Pareto Chart with cumulative line]
    PR -->|No| STK
```

---

## 4.4 Component Anatomy Block Diagram

This diagram shows the **seven mandatory components** of a bar chart, useful for KTU drawing-based questions.

```mermaid
graph LR
    T[Title at top center] --> XA[X axis label at bottom]
    XA --> XB[X axis tick marks and category names]
    XB --> YB[Y axis tick marks and numeric scale]
    YB --> YA[Y axis label on left side]
    YA --> BAR[Rectangular Bars equal width]
    BAR --> DL[Data Labels on top of bars]
    DL --> LG[Legend for grouped and stacked charts]
    LG --> RL[Reference line at mean value]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Conceptual Definition**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a Bar Chart. List any four key features that distinguish a Bar Chart from a Histogram.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Bar Chart** is a graphical representation of categorical data in which rectangular bars of equal width are drawn with lengths/heights proportional to the values they represent.

**Four distinguishing features vs. Histogram:**

1. **Data Type:** Bar chart uses **categorical (discrete) data**; histogram uses **continuous numerical data** grouped into intervals/bins.
2. **Bar Arrangement:** In a bar chart, bars are **separated by gaps**; in a histogram, bars are **touching each other** (no gaps) to indicate continuity.
3. **Axis Reordering:** In a bar chart, categories on the X-axis **can be reordered** arbitrarily; in a histogram, intervals **must remain in numerical order**.
4. **Variable Type:** Bar chart compares **nominal/ordinal** variables; histogram represents the **frequency distribution** of a single numerical variable.

---

### **Question 2: Construction Rule**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State the KTU-recommended rules for selecting the Y-axis scale in a Bar Chart. Why must the Y-axis always start at zero?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Rules for Y-axis selection:**

1. **Start at zero (0)** to avoid visual distortion of magnitudes. *(1 mark)*
2. **Upper bound** should be the smallest "nice" number greater than or equal to $V_{\max}$, where "nice" means a multiple of 1, 2, 5, or 10. *(1 mark)*
3. **Tick interval** should be chosen so that **6 to 8 ticks** appear on the axis (use $\Delta y = V_{\max}/7$ as a starting formula). *(1 mark)*

**Why Y-axis must start at zero:**
If the axis starts at a non-zero value (e.g., 20), then a bar of 60 will appear 6× taller than a bar of 30, even though it is only 2× the value. This **misleads the viewer** and violates the principle of **proportional representation** in data visualization. Hence, the KTU 2024 standard mandates a zero baseline.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B Module-3 questions in OEMET611 carry 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with **internal choice** between two question sets. Both alternatives are provided below for full preparation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Simple + Grouped Bar Chart**
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) The monthly sales (in ₹ Lakhs) of a company for the last 6 months are: Jan = 45, Feb = 52, Mar = 38, Apr = 60, May = 55, Jun = 70. Construct a Simple Vertical Bar Chart following the KTU OEMET611 construction procedure. Compute the mean, standard deviation, and draw a reference line. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1: Identify the variables.**
- X-axis: Month (Categorical, ordinal)
- Y-axis: Sales in ₹ Lakhs (Numerical)

**Step 2: Determine $V_{\max}$.**

$$V_{\max} = \max(45, 52, 38, 60, 55, 70) = 70$$

**Step 3: Determine tick interval.**

$$\Delta y = \frac{V_{\max}}{7} = \frac{70}{7} = 10$$

**Step 4: Y-axis range.**

$$Y_{\max} = 70, \quad \text{Ticks} = \{0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70\}$$

Total of **8 ticks** — within the 6–8 recommended range. ✓

**Step 5: Compute Mean.**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{45 + 52 + 38 + 60 + 55 + 70}{6} = \frac{320}{6} = 53.33 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}$$

**Step 6: Compute Standard Deviation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(V_i - \bar{x})^2} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{(45-53.33)^2 + (52-53.33)^2 + (38-53.33)^2 + (60-53.33)^2 + (55-53.33)^2 + (70-53.33)^2}{6}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{69.39 + 1.77 + 234.07 + 44.49 + 2.79 + 277.89}{6}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{630.40}{6}} = \sqrt{105.07} \approx 10.25 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Reference Line and Interpretation.**

- Reference line drawn at $y = 53.33$.
- **Above-average months:** Jan (no, 45 < 53.33), Feb (52 < 53.33, no), Mar (no), Apr (60 > 53.33, yes), May (55 > 53.33, yes), Jun (70 > 53.33, yes). **3 months above average.**
- **Outliers (using $|Z| > 2$):** $Z_{\text{Jun}} = (70 - 53.33)/10.25 = 1.63$ → Not an outlier. No extreme values.

**Step 8: Final chart** drawn with:
- Title: *"Monthly Sales Trend — First Half of 2025"*
- Y-axis: 0 to 70, ticks at 10-unit intervals
- Data labels displayed atop each bar
- Reference line at 53.33 drawn as a dashed red line, labeled "Mean = 53.33"

---

**(b) A retail chain compares Q1 and Q2 sales (₹ Lakhs) across 4 product categories: Electronics (Q1=80, Q2=95), Apparel (Q1=60, Q2=55), Groceries (Q1=45, Q2=50), Furniture (Q1=30, Q2=42). Construct a Grouped Bar Chart and explain the business insights. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1: Identify data.**

- 4 categories × 2 sub-groups = **8 bars total**.

**Step 2: Determine $V_{\max}$.**

$$V_{\max} = \max(80, 95, 60, 55, 45, 50, 30, 42) = 95$$

**Step 3: Tick interval.**

$$\Delta y = 95/7 \approx 13.57 \rightarrow \text{round to } 20$$

$$Y_{\max} = 100, \quad \text{Ticks} = \{0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100\}$$

5 ticks — slightly fewer than 6, but acceptable. (Use 10 to get 10 ticks; 6–8 is preferred, so 10 is too dense. 5 is acceptable in this case.)

**Step 4: Set up grouped layout.**

- Sub-bar width $W_{sb} = 1$ cm
- Inner gap $G_{inner} = 0.2$ cm
- Group width $= 2(1.0) + 0.2 = 2.2$ cm
- Inter-group gap $G_{group} = 1.0$ cm
- Total chart width $= 4(2.2) + 3(1.0) = 8.8 + 3.0 = 11.8$ cm

**Step 5: Compute growth percentages.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{\text{Electronics}} &= \frac{95-80}{80} \times 100 = +18.75\% \\
\Delta_{\text{Apparel}} &= \frac{55-60}{60} \times 100 = -8.33\% \\
\Delta_{\text{Groceries}} &= \frac{50-45}{45} \times 100 = +11.11\% \\
\Delta_{\text{Furniture}} &= \frac{42-30}{30} \times 100 = +40.00\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Business Insights.**

1. **Electronics** is the highest revenue driver in both quarters, growing steadily.
2. **Furniture** has the **highest growth rate (+40%)** — strong candidate for increased marketing investment.
3. **Apparel** shows a **decline (-8.33%)** — requires strategic intervention (promotions, restocking, or discontinuation analysis).
4. **Groceries** is stable with moderate growth — consistent performer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Stacked + 100% Stacked Bar Chart**
`[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a) A company tracks its quarterly revenue split between "Product A," "Product B," and "Product C" for two years (2023 and 2024). Data (₹ Crores): Q1 2023 = (A:30, B:20, C:10), Q2 2023 = (A:35, B:25, C:15), Q1 2024 = (A:40, B:30, C:20), Q2 2024 = (A:45, B:35, C:25). Construct a Stacked Bar Chart and a 100% Stacked Bar Chart. Compare. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute totals per quarter.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{Q1 2023}} &= 30 + 20 + 10 = 60 \\
T_{\text{Q2 2023}} &= 35 + 25 + 15 = 75 \\
T_{\text{Q1 2024}} &= 40 + 30 + 20 = 90 \\
T_{\text{Q2 2024}} &= 45 + 35 + 25 = 105 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: For Stacked Bar Chart:**

$$V_{\max} = 105, \quad \Delta y = 105/7 = 15 \rightarrow \text{round to } 20$$

$$Y_{\max} = 120, \quad \text{Ticks} = \{0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120\}$$

7 ticks — within recommended range. ✓

**Step 3: Compute percentage components for 100% Stacked Chart.**

For **Q1 2023** (Total = 60):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\%} &= 30/60 \times 100 = 50.00\% \\
B_{\%} &= 20/60 \times 100 = 33.33\% \\
C_{\%} &= 10/60 \times 100 = 16.67\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Q2 2023** (Total = 75):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\%} &= 35/75 \times 100 = 46.67\% \\
B_{\%} &= 25/75 \times 100 = 33.33\% \\
C_{\%} &= 15/75 \times 100 = 20.00\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Q1 2024** (Total = 90):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\%} &= 40/90 \times 100 = 44.44\% \\
B_{\%} &= 30/90 \times 100 = 33.33\% \\
C_{\%} &= 20/90 \times 100 = 22.22\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Q2 2024** (Total = 105):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\%} &= 45/105 \times 100 = 42.86\% \\
B_{\%} &= 35/105 \times 100 = 33.33\% \\
C_{\%} &= 25/105 \times 100 = 23.81\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verification — each row sums to 100%:** All verified ✓.

**Step 5: Business Insights.**

- **Absolute revenue is growing** across all quarters (60 → 75 → 90 → 105).
- **Product A's share is declining** proportionally (50% → 46.67% → 44.44% → 42.86%), even though absolute revenue is increasing.
- **Product B's share is stable** at 33.33% across all quarters — a reliable "anchor" product.
- **Product C is the rising star** in proportion (16.67% → 23.81%) — diversification is improving.

---

**(b) What is a Pareto Chart? How is it constructed from a Bar Chart? Using the data: Defects — Type A=50, Type B=15, Type C=25, Type D=10 — construct a Pareto chart and identify the "vital few." (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition of Pareto Chart.**

A **Pareto Chart** is a special type of bar chart where bars are sorted in **descending order** of frequency/value, and a **cumulative percentage line** is overlaid on top. It is based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**, which states that roughly 80% of effects come from 20% of causes.

**Step 2: Sort the defects in descending order.**

| Defect Type | Count |
|-------------|------:|
| A | 50 |
| C | 25 |
| B | 15 |
| D | 10 |

Total: $50 + 25 + 15 + 10 = 100$

**Step 3: Compute cumulative percentage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A &= \frac{50}{100} \times 100 = 50\% \\
C_C &= \frac{50+25}{100} \times 100 = 75\% \\
C_B &= \frac{50+25+15}{100} \times 100 = 90\% \\
C_D &= \frac{50+25+15+10}{100} \times 100 = 100\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Construct the chart.**

- **Bars:** Defects A (50), C (25), B (15), D (10) in descending order.
- **Cumulative line:** Plotted against a secondary Y-axis (0–100%) at points (A, 50%), (C, 75%), (B, 90%), (D, 100%).

**Step 5: Identify the "vital few".**

The first **2 defect types (A and C)** account for **75%** of all defects. This is **close to 80%**, meaning defects A and C are the **"vital few"** that the quality team should prioritize for corrective action. Defects B and D together form the **"trivial many."**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Bar Chart Questions:**
>
> 1. **Y-axis not starting at zero (–2 marks):** Many students truncate the Y-axis to "make differences look bigger." This is a **misleading practice** and KTU examiners deduct marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing histogram with bar chart (–2 marks):** Using a bar chart for continuous data, or vice versa, is treated as a **conceptual error**.
>
> 3. **Incorrect denominator in 100% stacked chart (–3 marks):** Using the grand total instead of the column total is the **#1 most common mistake** in OEMET611.
>
> 4. **Missing data labels or axis labels (–1 to –2 marks):** Even a perfectly drawn chart loses marks if the axes are unlabeled or units are missing.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the 50% bar-gap rule (–1 mark):** Bars that are too close or too far apart violate the KTU construction standard.
>
> 6. **3D effects / decorative elements (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU 2024 OEMET611 explicitly discourages 3D bars, shadows, and unnecessary decorations.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the reference line or mean value (–1 mark):** For analytical questions, omitting the mean reference line is a deduction.
>
> 8. **Wrong tick interval (–1 mark):** Using 1, 3, 7, or other "ugly" numbers as tick intervals is penalized.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Bar Charts (OEMET611 Module 3)**

- **Definition:** A Bar Chart is a **categorical data visualization** using **rectangular bars** with heights/lengths proportional to values.
- **Five Types:** Simple, Horizontal, Grouped, Stacked, 100% Stacked.
- **Y-axis must start at 0** — non-negotiable KTU rule.
- **Bar width is uniform** across all bars.
- **Inter-bar gap = 50% of bar width** (KTU standard).
- **Tick interval:** Use 6–8 ticks on Y-axis; choose "nice" numbers (1, 2, 5, 10).
- **Sort bars descending** in simple bar charts unless time order matters.
- **Grouped chart** for comparing sub-variables; **stacked** for totals + parts.
- **100% stacked formula:** $V_{ij}^{\%} = V_{ij} / T_j \times 100$ (denominator = **column total**, not grand total).
- **Pareto chart** = sorted bar + cumulative % line; follows the **80/20 rule**.
- **Histogram ≠ Bar Chart** — continuous vs. discrete data.
- **Mean reference line:** $y = \bar{x}$ for analytical comparison.
- **Standard deviation overlay:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum(V_i - \bar{x})^2}$.
- **Color rule:** Use **color-blind friendly palettes**; avoid red-green only.
- **Avoid 3D bars** — they distort perception and are penalized in KTU exams.
- **Aspect ratio:** 1.6:1 (width:height) is the KTU-recommended default.
- **Legend mandatory** for grouped/stacked charts; optional for simple charts.
- **Data labels recommended** on top of each bar for precise reading.
- **Outlier detection rule:** $\vert Z_i \vert > 2$ flags an outlier for analytical review.
- **Min-Max normalization** rescales bars to [0, 1] when comparing variables of different units.
- **Pareto cumulative formula:** $C_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i} \times 100$.
- **KTU 2024 OEMET611 marks distribution:** Part A = 2 × 3 = 6 marks; Part B = 1 × 14 = 14 marks (out of module total 20 marks for this topic).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pie Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET611 - INTRODUCTION TO BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pie Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pie Chart** is a circular statistical graphic divided into proportional sectors (or "slices") that numerically represent categories of data as proportions of the whole. The entire circle represents **100% of the data set (the total)**, while each slice's central angle is directly proportional to the percentage that category contributes to the total.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for **Business Analytics**, the pie chart belongs to the family of **Part-to-Whole (Compositional) Charts** and is classified under **Univariate Categorical Visualization**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A pie chart is considered the *primary qualitative data visualization tool* when the analytical objective is to demonstrate the **composition** of a single categorical variable at one point in time.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Pizza Analogy** 🍕
>
> Imagine you order a large pizza for a party of friends. The whole pizza represents your **total budget of ₹10,000** for the month. You then "slice" the pizza into wedges — rent gets a large slice, food a medium slice, transport a smaller slice, and entertainment the smallest slice. The **bigger the slice**, the **bigger the proportion** of that expense relative to your total budget.
>
> A pie chart works **exactly the same way**: the *full circle* is the *total*, and each *slice* shows what fraction of that total each category takes up.

### 1.3 Core Mathematical Constant

The **fundamental constant** governing every pie chart is the angle of a full circle:

$$360^{\circ} \equiv 100\% \equiv 1 \text{ Whole (Total)}$$

This equivalence is the mathematical anchor from which every slice angle is derived.

### 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| Slice / Sector | One individual wedge of the circle representing one category |
| Central Angle | The angle subtended at the centre by a slice (measured in degrees) |
| Proportion | The fractional share of the total that one category represents |
| Legend | The color-coded key identifying which slice represents which category |
| Clockwise Convention | Standard practice — slices are arranged starting from **12 o'clock (0°/360°)** position |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

The construction of a pie chart follows a precise 5-step logic chain:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Variable:** Confirm the data is *categorical* (e.g., product type, region, department) and that the analytical question demands a *part-to-whole* comparison.
- **Step 2 — Compute the Total:** Sum all category values to obtain $T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$.
- **Step 3 — Convert Values to Proportions:** Calculate the proportion $p_i$ of each category $i$ using $p_i = x_i / T$.
- **Step 4 — Convert Proportions to Angles:** Multiply each proportion by **360°** to obtain the central angle $\theta_i$.
- **Step 5 — Draw and Label:** Construct the circle starting from the 12 o'clock mark and draw each slice with its computed angle. Label each slice with its percentage and category name; use a legend for color coding.

> [!TIP]
> **Why 360°?** Because ancient Babylonians used a base-60 (sexagesimal) number system, which gave us 60 seconds, 60 minutes, and **360° in a circle**. Any consistent full-circle value works mathematically (e.g., 2π radians), but 360° is the universal standard for pie charts.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit |
|---|----------------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $\theta_i = \left(\dfrac{x_i}{T}\right) \times 360^{\circ}$ | **Master Angle Formula** — converts a raw value into a slice angle | degrees (°) |
| 2 | $\%_i = \left(\dfrac{x_i}{T}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage contribution of category $i$ | percent (%) |
| 3 | $\theta_i = \dfrac{\%_i}{100} \times 360^{\circ}$ | Alternative form using percentage | degrees (°) |
| 4 | $T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Total of all categories | units of data |
| 5 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_i = 360^{\circ}$ | Verification rule — all angles must sum to 360° | degrees (°) |
| 6 | $p_i = \dfrac{x_i}{T}$ | Proportion (decimal fraction) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = 1$ | Proportions sum to unity | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Validation Rule:** The sum of all central angles **must equal exactly 360°**. If it doesn't, a calculation error exists. KTU examiners specifically check this.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

In business analytics practice, pie charts are deployed across:

- **Marketing Analytics:** Market share distribution among competing brands.
- **HR Analytics:** Workforce composition by department, gender, or qualification.
- **Operations Management:** Breakdown of total cost (materials, labor, overhead, profit).
- **Financial Reporting:** Allocation of the budgeted revenue across business units.
- **Healthcare Analytics:** Distribution of patient categories by disease type in a hospital.
- **Energy Sector:** Composition of India's electricity generation mix (coal, hydro, solar, wind, nuclear).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Angle Formula

The pie chart is a direct mapping from a *proportion of total* to a *proportion of a circle's angular measure*. Let us derive this rigorously.

**Given:**
- A full circle subtends an angle of $360^{\circ}$ at its centre.
- A full circle also represents the *total* $T$ of the data set.
- Category $i$ has a value $x_i$, contributing a fraction of the total.

**Step 1 — Establish Direct Proportionality:**

We assert that the central angle $\theta_i$ of category $i$ is *directly proportional* to the value $x_i$ of that category:

$$\theta_i \propto x_i$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the Constant of Proportionality:**

Using $T$ as the total value and $360^{\circ}$ as the total angle:

$$\theta_i = k \cdot x_i$$

When $x_i = T$, then $\theta_i = 360^{\circ}$. Substituting:

$$360^{\circ} = k \cdot T \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Back:**

$$\boxed{\theta_i = \dfrac{x_i}{T} \times 360^{\circ}}$$

This is the **Master Angle Formula**, valid for every pie chart construction.

**Step 4 — Verification of the Sum Rule:**

Summing across all $n$ categories:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{x_i}{T} \times 360^{\circ} = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{T} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{T} \cdot T = 360^{\circ} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Pattern

> **Question:** A company spends its monthly revenue of **₹5,00,000** across four heads: Raw Materials ₹2,00,000; Salaries ₹1,50,000; Rent ₹75,000; and Miscellaneous ₹75,000. Construct the pie chart and present the central angles.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Verify the Total.**

$$T = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 75{,}000 + 75{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2: Compute the Angle for Raw Materials.**

$$\theta_{\text{RM}} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 360^{\circ} = \dfrac{2}{5} \times 360^{\circ} = 144^{\circ}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Angle for Salaries.**

$$\theta_{\text{Sal}} = \dfrac{1{,}50{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 360^{\circ} = \dfrac{3}{10} \times 360^{\circ} = 108^{\circ}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Angle for Rent.**

$$\theta_{\text{Rent}} = \dfrac{75{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 360^{\circ} = \dfrac{3}{20} \times 360^{\circ} = 54^{\circ}$$

**Step 5: Compute the Angle for Miscellaneous.**

$$\theta_{\text{Misc}} = \dfrac{75{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 360^{\circ} = 54^{\circ}$$

**Step 6: Validate the Sum.**

$$\sum \theta = 144^{\circ} + 108^{\circ} + 54^{\circ} + 54^{\circ} = 360^{\circ} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Analytical Computation Matrix

| Category | Value $x_i$ (₹) | Proportion $p_i = x_i / T$ | Percentage $\%_i$ | Central Angle $\theta_i$ (°) |
|----------|-----------------|----------------------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| Raw Materials | 2,00,000 | 0.400 | 40.0% | 144.0° |
| Salaries | 1,50,000 | 0.300 | 30.0% | 108.0° |
| Rent | 75,000 | 0.150 | 15.0% | 54.0° |
| Miscellaneous | 75,000 | 0.150 | 15.0% | 54.0° |
| **Total** | **5,00,000** | **1.000** | **100.0%** | **360.0°** |

### 3.4 Conversion Identity — Angle ↔ Percentage

Since percentage is just proportion scaled by 100, an elegant shortcut exists:

$$\theta_i = \%_i \times 3.6^{\circ}$$

Because $360^{\circ} / 100 = 3.6^{\circ}$ per percentage point. For example, $40\% \times 3.6 = 144^{\circ}$, which matches Step 2 above. This is a rapid-verification tool for KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pie Chart Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Categorical Data Input] --> B[Step 1: Compute Total T = sum of x_i]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Proportions p_i = x_i over T]
    C --> D[Step 3: Convert to Angles theta_i = p_i times 360]
    D --> E[Step 4: Rank Slices Descending by Angle]
    E --> F[Step 5: Draw Circle and Mark 12 Oclock Start]
    F --> G[Step 6: Lay Out Slices Clockwise in Rank Order]
    G --> H[Step 7: Label Each Slice with Percentage and Name]
    H --> I[Step 8: Add Color Coding and Legend]
    I --> J[Final Validated Pie Chart Output]

    V1[Validation Check 1: Sum of angles = 360] -.-> J
    V2[Validation Check 2: Categories are mutually exclusive] -.-> B
    V3[Validation Check 3: Total is non-zero] -.-> B
```

### 4.2 Schematic Layout of the Worked Example

```mermaid
pie title Monthly Expense Distribution of a Company (Total Rs 5,00,000)
    "Raw Materials : 144 degrees" : 200000
    "Salaries : 108 degrees" : 150000
    "Rent : 54 degrees" : 75000
    "Miscellaneous : 54 degrees" : 75000
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — When to Use a Pie Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{Is the data categorical?} -->|Yes| Q2{Total of n categories = 100 percent?}
    Q1 -->|No| NX1[Use Histogram or Bar Chart instead]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Number of categories <= 7?}
    Q2 -->|No| NX2[Use Bar Chart instead]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Are all values strictly positive?}
    Q3 -->|No| NX3[Group smallest into Others slice]
    Q4 -->|Yes| USE[Use Pie Chart - Ideal Choice]
    Q4 -->|No| NX4[Use Stacked Bar Chart instead]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Anatomy — Pie Chart vs Donut Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIE[Pie Chart Core Features] --> PIE1[Solid circular disc]
    PIE --> PIE2[Each slice = one category]
    PIE --> PIE3[Total area encodes 100 percent]
    PIE --> PIE4[Center has no usable space]

    DON[Donut Chart Core Features] --> DON1[Hollow center band]
    DON --> DON2[Same angle logic as pie]
    DON --> DON3[Center can hold KPI text]
    DON --> DON4[Modern variant for dashboards]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
Define a Pie Chart. State any **two situations** where it is the most appropriate visualization tool.

**Model Answer:**

A pie chart is a circular diagram divided into sectors, where each sector's central angle is proportional to the category's share of the total. The whole circle equals 100% of the data.

**Two appropriate situations:**
1. When displaying the **composition of a budget** (e.g., revenue allocation across departments).
2. When comparing **market share** of competing brands in an industry snapshot.

*(Accept any valid part-to-whole scenario: workforce composition, expense breakdown, demographic distribution.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper]**
The central angle of a slice in a pie chart is **72°**. What is the **percentage contribution** of that category to the total?

**Model Answer:**

Using the conversion identity $\theta_i = \%_i \times 3.6^{\circ}$:

$$\%_i = \dfrac{\theta_i}{3.6^{\circ}} = \dfrac{72^{\circ}}{3.6^{\circ}} = 20\%$$

**The category contributes 20% of the total.** *(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper] — Question A (14 Marks)**

The following data represents the **distribution of monthly household expenditure** of a family in Kerala:

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|------|------------|
| Food | 12,000 |
| Rent | 10,000 |
| Education | 8,000 |
| Transport | 6,000 |
| Healthcare | 4,000 |
| Miscellaneous | 10,000 |
| **Total** | **50,000** |

**(a)** Compute the central angle for each category using the master angle formula. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Draw the pie chart representation and explain what insights can be drawn from it. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

Apply the master formula $\theta_i = \dfrac{x_i}{T} \times 360^{\circ}$ for each category with $T = 50{,}000$.

$$\theta_{\text{Food}} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 360^{\circ} = 0.24 \times 360^{\circ} = 86.4^{\circ}$$

$$\theta_{\text{Rent}} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 360^{\circ} = 0.20 \times 360^{\circ} = 72.0^{\circ}$$

$$\theta_{\text{Education}} = \dfrac{8{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 360^{\circ} = 0.16 \times 360^{\circ} = 57.6^{\circ}$$

$$\theta_{\text{Transport}} = \dfrac{6{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 360^{\circ} = 0.12 \times 360^{\circ} = 43.2^{\circ}$$

$$\theta_{\text{Healthcare}} = \dfrac{4{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 360^{\circ} = 0.08 \times 360^{\circ} = 28.8^{\circ}$$

$$\theta_{\text{Misc}} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 360^{\circ} = 0.20 \times 360^{\circ} = 72.0^{\circ}$$

**Validation:**

$$\sum \theta = 86.4 + 72.0 + 57.6 + 43.2 + 28.8 + 72.0 = 360.0^{\circ} \quad \checkmark$$

**Computation Matrix:**

| Item | Value $x_i$ (₹) | Proportion $p_i$ | Percentage | Angle $\theta_i$ |
|------|-----------------|------------------|------------|------------------|
| Food | 12,000 | 0.24 | 24% | 86.4° |
| Rent | 10,000 | 0.20 | 20% | 72.0° |
| Education | 8,000 | 0.16 | 16% | 57.6° |
| Transport | 6,000 | 0.12 | 12% | 43.2° |
| Healthcare | 4,000 | 0.08 | 8% | 28.8° |
| Miscellaneous | 10,000 | 0.20 | 20% | 72.0° |
| **Total** | **50,000** | **1.00** | **100%** | **360.0°** |

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

The pie chart is constructed by drawing a circle and marking the computed angles clockwise starting from the 12 o'clock position:

```mermaid
pie title Monthly Household Expenditure Distribution (Total Rs 50,000)
    "Food 86.4 degrees" : 12000
    "Rent 72.0 degrees" : 10000
    "Education 57.6 degrees" : 8000
    "Transport 43.2 degrees" : 6000
    "Healthcare 28.8 degrees" : 4000
    "Miscellaneous 72.0 degrees" : 10000
```

**Insights Drawn:**
1. **Food** consumes the **largest share (24%)** of the family's monthly budget, reflecting Kerala's relatively high cost of living for essential groceries.
2. **Rent and Miscellaneous** tie for the second-largest share at **20% each**, together accounting for 40% of expenses.
3. **Healthcare** has the **smallest share (8%)**, indicating either good current health status or potential under-investment in medical preparedness.
4. **Essential needs** (Food + Rent + Education + Healthcare) total **68%**, leaving only **32%** for discretionary and miscellaneous spending — a typical middle-class Kerala household pattern.

---

**Q3 Alt. — Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice)*

The annual sales (in ₹ Lakhs) of 5 products of a company are: Product A — 80; Product B — 120; Product C — 60; Product D — 100; Product E — 40.

**(a)** Construct the data table with proportion, percentage, and central angle for each product. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Discuss **three advantages** and **two limitations** of using a pie chart for this data. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

Total sales $T = 80 + 120 + 60 + 100 + 40 = 400$ Lakhs.

| Product | Sales $x_i$ (₹ Lakh) | $p_i = x_i / T$ | Percentage | $\theta_i$ (°) |
|---------|----------------------|-----------------|------------|----------------|
| A | 80 | 0.20 | 20% | 72.0° |
| B | 120 | 0.30 | 30% | 108.0° |
| C | 60 | 0.15 | 15% | 54.0° |
| D | 100 | 0.25 | 25% | 90.0° |
| E | 40 | 0.10 | 10% | 36.0° |
| **Total** | **400** | **1.00** | **100%** | **360.0°** |

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Three Advantages:**
1. **Intuitive Part-to-Whole Visualization:** A viewer can instantly grasp that Product B is the top contributor (30%) versus Product E being the smallest (10%) without reading any numbers.
2. **Easy Comparison of Dominant Categories:** With only 5 categories, the eye can quickly rank slices by size, identifying Product B as the market leader and Product E as the laggard.
3. **Effective for Non-Technical Audiences:** Pie charts require no statistical literacy and are universally understood in boardroom presentations.

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Difficult Precise Comparison of Similar Slices:** Product A (72°) and Product C (54°) are visually distinct, but if two products had similar percentages (e.g., 19% and 21%), the eye cannot reliably differentiate — a bar chart would be superior.
2. **Scalability Issues:** If the company expanded to 15 products, the pie chart would become cluttered and unreadable. Pie charts ideally work with **5–7 categories maximum**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** write "pie chart is best for comparing data" — it is best for **part-to-whole composition only**. Examiners deduct marks for this misconception.
> - **Always** verify that $\sum \theta_i = 360^{\circ}$ in your answer. Showing this validation earns a free mark.
> - **Avoid** confusing "percentage" with "angle" in your final step. They are different units. Always state both.
> - **Do not** use a pie chart when categories exceed 7 — explicitly mention this rule to score on the limitations sub-part.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Formula:** $\theta_i = \dfrac{x_i}{T} \times 360^{\circ}$ — memorize this as the **single most important equation**.
- **Identity Check:** $1\% \equiv 3.6^{\circ}$. Use this for rapid verification.
- **Sum Rule:** All slice angles must sum to **exactly 360°** — examiners verify this.
- **Start Position:** Always begin drawing from the **12 o'clock position** and move **clockwise** (standard convention).
- **Optimal Category Count:** Pie charts work best with **5–7 categories**. Beyond 7, switch to a bar chart.
- **Data Type Restriction:** Use pie charts only for **categorical (qualitative) part-to-whole** data, never for continuous or time-series data.
- **All values must be non-negative:** Negative or zero values cannot be represented as a slice.
- **Label every slice:** Always include **percentage** and **category name** directly on or beside each slice.
- **Sort by size:** Arrange slices from **largest to smallest** in clockwise direction for instant readability.
- **Total must be the sum of all parts:** Pie charts assume a closed total (no "missing" categories).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scatter Plot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET611 - INTRODUCTION TO BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scatter Plot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Scatter Plot (Scatter Diagram / Scatter Graph)**
> A **Scatter Plot** is a two-dimensional graphical data visualization technique that uses **Cartesian coordinates** to display the joint distribution of two numerical variables. Each observation in the dataset is represented as a single point $(x_i, y_i)$ on a rectangular plotting area, where the horizontal axis denotes the **independent (explanatory) variable** and the vertical axis denotes the **dependent (response) variable**. It is the foundational exploratory data analysis (EDA) tool for studying the **strength, direction, and form of bivariate relationships** before any formal statistical modeling is attempted.

In the KTU 2024 Business Analytics framework, the scatter plot is positioned as the **pre-cursor to correlation analysis and linear regression**, allowing analysts to visually hypothesize the underlying functional relationship $Y = f(X) + \varepsilon$ between two quantitative business metrics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Stargazer's Telescope" Analogy**
> Imagine you are an astronomer scanning the night sky. Each star you observe has two recorded properties: its **brightness (magnitude)** and its **distance from Earth**. If you plot every star as a single dot — brightness on one axis, distance on the other — a clear pattern often emerges: the brightest stars tend to be closer, while dimmer stars lie at varying distances. The scatter of points tells the story of how these two properties move together.
> 
> **In business terms:** Replace "brightness" with *advertising spend* and "distance" with *monthly revenue*. The scatter plot instantly reveals whether money spent on ads genuinely translates into higher revenue. No complex table of numbers, no equations — just a single, intuitive picture.

### 1.3 Standard Variables & Metrics Used

In a KTU assessment context, the following terminology is **mandatory**:

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Independent Variable | $X$ | Predictor / Explanatory / Regressor |
| Dependent Variable | $Y$ | Response / Outcome / Predicted |
| Observation | $(x_i, y_i)$ | A single data point for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$ |
| Correlation Strength | $\vert r \vert$ | Magnitude of linear association |
| Correlation Direction | $\text{sign}(r)$ | Positive (+) or Negative (−) |
| Outlier | $(x_o, y_o)$ | A point deviating sharply from the cluster |

> **Bold Constants for KTU Recall:** The **Pearson correlation coefficient** is conventionally denoted by **$r$**, ranges from **$-1 \le r \le +1$**, and is **dimensionless** (unit-free).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Construction — Step-by-Step Logic

The construction of a scatter plot follows a strict, sequential procedure used by data analysts worldwide:

1. **Variable Identification** — Determine which business variable is the *cause* (independent, $X$) and which is the *effect* (dependent, $Y$). Example: $X$ = Price, $Y$ = Units Sold.
2. **Data Tabulation** — Collect $n$ paired observations and arrange them in a two-column dataset.
3. **Axis Calibration** — Choose equal, evenly-spaced numerical scales on both $X$ and $Y$ axes. The scale must start at or below the minimum observed value and end at or above the maximum.
4. **Point Plotting** — For each row $i$, place a dot (or marker) at the precise Cartesian coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$.
5. **Title & Labelling** — Add a descriptive title, axis labels with **units**, and a legend if multiple series exist.
6. **Pattern Recognition** — Visually inspect the cloud of points to detect trends, clusters, gaps, and outliers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Examiners often award **2 marks** simply for correctly labelled axes with units. A scatter plot without axis units is considered an *incomplete* diagram and may attract partial deduction.

### 2.2 The Three Canonical Patterns of Association

| Pattern | Visual Signature | Business Interpretation | Correlation $r$ |
|---------|------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **Positive Correlation** | Points slope upward from lower-left to upper-right | As $X$ rises, $Y$ rises (e.g., Ad Spend $\uparrow$ → Sales $\uparrow$) | $0 < r \le +1$ |
| **Negative Correlation** | Points slope downward from upper-left to lower-right | As $X$ rises, $Y$ falls (e.g., Price $\uparrow$ → Demand $\downarrow$) | $-1 \le r < 0$ |
| **No Correlation** | Points form a random, structure-less cloud | No linear relationship between $X$ and $Y$ | $r \approx 0$ |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Board Examinations**

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Pearson Correlation Coefficient | $r = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Range: $-1 \le r \le +1$ |
| 2 | Computational Form of $r$ | $r = \dfrac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{\sqrt{[n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2][n\sum y_i^2 - (\sum y_i)^2]}}$ | Easier for raw data |
| 3 | Slope of Regression Line | $b = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | "Rise over Run" of best fit |
| 4 | Intercept of Regression Line | $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$ | Point where line crosses $Y$-axis |
| 5 | Equation of Best-Fit Line | $\hat{y} = a + bx$ | Used for prediction |
| 6 | Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = r^2$ | Proportion of variance explained |
| 7 | Outlier Threshold (Z-Score) | $\vert z_i \vert = \dfrac{\vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{s_x} > 3$ | Rule-of-thumb for flagging outliers |

> **Real-World Utility:** In modern business intelligence (BI) platforms such as **Power BI, Tableau, and Looker**, the scatter plot is the default visual for **drill-down analysis** during root-cause investigations, **A/B testing validation**, and **customer segmentation** (e.g., plotting *Age* vs. *Annual Spend* to identify high-value clusters).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Constructing and Interpreting a Scatter Plot

**Problem Statement:**
A retail manager records the following data for 8 weeks relating **advertising expenditure** (in ₹ thousands) to **weekly sales revenue** (in ₹ lakhs). Construct the scatter plot, compute the Pearson correlation coefficient, fit the regression line, and predict sales for a spend of ₹15,000.

| Week ($i$) | Ad Spend $x_i$ (₹ '000s) | Sales $y_i$ (₹ lakhs) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 2 | 5 |
| 2 | 4 | 6 |
| 3 | 4 | 7 |
| 4 | 5 | 8 |
| 5 | 6 | 9 |
| 6 | 7 | 10 |
| 7 | 8 | 12 |
| 8 | 10 | 13 |

**Step 1 — Compute the Sums ($n = 8$):**

$$\sum x_i = 2 + 4 + 4 + 5 + 6 + 7 + 8 + 10 = 46$$

$$\sum y_i = 5 + 6 + 7 + 8 + 9 + 10 + 12 + 13 = 70$$

$$\sum x_i^2 = 4 + 16 + 16 + 25 + 36 + 49 + 64 + 100 = 310$$

$$\sum y_i^2 = 25 + 36 + 49 + 64 + 81 + 100 + 144 + 169 = 668$$

$$\sum x_i y_i = (2)(5) + (4)(6) + (4)(7) + (5)(8) + (6)(9) + (7)(10) + (8)(12) + (10)(13)$$

Let us expand this product explicitly to demonstrate the computational rigor required at the KTU board level:

$$\sum x_i y_i = 10 + 24 + 28 + 40 + 54 + 70 + 96 + 130 = 452$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Means:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \frac{46}{8} = 5.75$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{\sum y_i}{n} = \frac{70}{8} = 8.75$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Computational Formula for Pearson's $r$:**

$$r = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{\sqrt{[n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2][n \sum y_i^2 - (\sum y_i)^2]}}$$

Substituting the calculated values into the numerator:

$$r_{\text{num}} = (8)(452) - (46)(70) = 3616 - 3220 = 396$$

Now the denominator components:

$$r_{\text{den, A}} = 8(310) - (46)^2 = 2480 - 2116 = 364$$

$$r_{\text{den, B}} = 8(668) - (70)^2 = 5344 - 4900 = 444$$

The full denominator is the geometric mean of these two quantities:

$$r_{\text{den}} = \sqrt{(364)(444)} = \sqrt{161616} \approx 402.0149$$

Finally, the correlation coefficient:

$$r = \frac{396}{402.0149} \approx +0.9850$$

> **Interpretation:** Since $r \approx +0.985$ is **very close to +1**, the data exhibits an **extremely strong positive linear correlation** between advertising expenditure and sales revenue.

**Step 4 — Compute the Slope ($b$) of the Regression Line:**

$$b = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} = \frac{396}{364} \approx 1.0879$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Intercept ($a$):**

$$a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x} = 8.75 - (1.0879)(5.75) = 8.75 - 6.2554 \approx 2.4946$$

**Step 6 — The Best-Fit Regression Equation:**

$$\hat{y} = 2.4946 + 1.0879\,x$$

**Step 7 — Predict Sales for Ad Spend $x = 15$ (₹ '000s):**

$$\hat{y} = 2.4946 + (1.0879)(15) = 2.4946 + 16.3185 = 18.8131$$

> **Predicted Sales Revenue** $\approx$ **₹18.81 lakhs** when ₹15,000 is spent on advertising.

**Step 8 — Compute the Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$):**

$$R^2 = r^2 = (0.9850)^2 \approx 0.9702$$

> **Interpretation:** Approximately **97.02%** of the variation in weekly sales is explained by the variation in advertising spend — an exceptionally strong predictive model for business decision-making.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Outlier Detection Logic

| Condition | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| $\vert z_i \vert \le 2$ | Point lies within the **normal band** — retain |
| $2 < \vert z_i \vert \le 3$ | Point is a **mild outlier** — investigate |
| $\vert z_i \vert > 3$ | Point is an **extreme outlier** — consider removal or robust methods |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Scatter Plot Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Bivariate Business Data] --> B[Identify X Independent and Y Dependent Variables]
    B --> C[Tabulate n Paired Observations]
    C --> D[Calibrate Cartesian Axes with Units]
    D --> E[Plot Each Point as x_i, y_i]
    E --> F[Visual Pattern Recognition]
    F --> G{What Shape Emerges?}
    G -->|Upward Slope| H[Positive Correlation r greater than 0]
    G -->|Downward Slope| I[Negative Correlation r less than 0]
    G -->|Random Cloud| J[No Linear Correlation r approx 0]
    H --> K[Compute Pearson r and r squared]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Consider Non-Linear Models]
    K --> M[Fit Least Squares Line y hat equals a plus bx]
    M --> N[Check for Outliers via Z-Score]
    N --> O[Make Business Predictions and Decisions]
    L --> O
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Scatter Plot System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Data Layer]
        DB[(Business Database)]
        CSV[CSV or Excel File]
        API[Live API Stream]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer[Processing Engine]
        Clean[Data Cleaning Module]
        Calc[Statistical Calculator]
        Corr[Correlation Engine r]
        Reg[Regression Engine a, b]
    end

    subgraph VisualizationLayer[Visualization Output]
        Plot[Scatter Plot Canvas]
        Trend[Trendline Overlay]
        Marker[Outlier Highlighter]
        Tooltip[Interactive Tooltip]
    end

    subgraph DecisionLayer[Business Decision Layer]
        Forecast[Revenue Forecast]
        Segment[Customer Segmentation]
        Anomaly[Anomaly Detection]
    end

    DB --> Clean
    CSV --> Clean
    API --> Clean
    Clean --> Calc
    Calc --> Corr
    Calc --> Reg
    Corr --> Plot
    Reg --> Trend
    Calc --> Marker
    Plot --> Tooltip
    Trend --> Forecast
    Plot --> Segment
    Marker --> Anomaly
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Data flows from the **Input Layer** (raw sources) through the **Processing Engine** (statistical computation) into the **Visualization Output** (the scatter plot itself) and finally informs the **Business Decision Layer** where actionable insights are produced.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a scatter plot. Mention any two business scenarios where it is the most appropriate visualization tool.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A scatter plot is a two-dimensional graphical representation that displays the relationship between two numerical variables by plotting each observation as a point in the Cartesian plane, with the independent variable on the $X$-axis and the dependent variable on the $Y$-axis.

**Two business scenarios:**
1. **Marketing Analytics** — Plotting advertising spend (₹) against sales revenue (₹) to determine the effectiveness of marketing campaigns.
2. **Human Resources** — Plotting employee training hours against performance rating scores to assess return on training investment.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each business scenario = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the range and interpretation of the Pearson correlation coefficient $r$. What does a value of $r = 0$ signify?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Range:** The Pearson correlation coefficient $r$ lies in the closed interval $-1 \le r \le +1$. (1 mark)
- **Interpretation of extremes:** $r = +1$ indicates a perfect positive linear relationship; $r = -1$ indicates a perfect negative linear relationship. (1 mark)
- **Meaning of $r = 0$:** A value of $r = 0$ signifies that there is **no linear correlation** between the two variables. The points form a random cloud with no discernible linear trend. (1 mark)

> **Note:** $r = 0$ does **not** imply that no relationship exists — it only means the relationship, if any, is **non-linear** (e.g., quadratic, exponential).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** The following data shows the relationship between years of experience ($X$) and monthly salary in ₹ thousands ($Y$) for 6 employees:

| Employee | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Experience $x_i$ (years) | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| Salary $y_i$ (₹ '000) | 25 | 30 | 35 | 40 | 50 | 55 |

**(i)** Construct the scatter plot and comment on the nature of the relationship. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Compute the Pearson correlation coefficient $r$. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** **(i)** Fit a least-squares regression line of $Y$ on $X$. **(4 marks)**
**(ii)** Predict the salary of an employee with 8 years of experience. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) Scatter Plot & Comment (3 marks):**

On plotting $(2,25), (3,30), (4,35), (5,40), (6,50), (7,55)$ on a Cartesian plane, the points form a **clear upward-sloping pattern** moving from lower-left to upper-right. **Comment:** There exists a **strong positive linear correlation** between experience and salary — as experience increases, salary also increases.

**(a) (ii) Compute $r$ (4 marks):**

Tabulating the required sums ($n = 6$):

$$\sum x_i = 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6 + 7 = 27$$
$$\sum y_i = 25 + 30 + 35 + 40 + 50 + 55 = 235$$
$$\sum x_i^2 = 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 36 + 49 = 139$$
$$\sum y_i^2 = 625 + 900 + 1225 + 1600 + 2500 + 3025 = 9875$$
$$\sum x_i y_i = 50 + 90 + 140 + 200 + 300 + 385 = 1165$$

Applying the computational formula:

$$r = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{\sqrt{[n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2][n \sum y_i^2 - (\sum y_i)^2]}}$$

$$r = \frac{6(1165) - (27)(235)}{\sqrt{[6(139) - 729][6(9875) - 55225]}}$$

$$r = \frac{6990 - 6345}{\sqrt{[834 - 729][59250 - 55225]}}$$

$$r = \frac{645}{\sqrt{(105)(4025)}}$$

$$r = \frac{645}{\sqrt{422625}} = \frac{645}{650.10} \approx +0.9923$$

**Interpretation:** $r \approx +0.99$ indicates a **very strong positive linear correlation**.

**(b) (i) Least-Squares Regression Line (4 marks):**

Slope:

$$b = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} = \frac{645}{105} \approx 6.1429$$

Means: $\bar{x} = 27/6 = 4.5$, $\bar{y} = 235/6 \approx 39.1667$

Intercept:

$$a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x} = 39.1667 - (6.1429)(4.5) = 39.1667 - 27.6429 \approx 11.5238$$

**Regression equation:**

$$\hat{y} = 11.5238 + 6.1429\,x$$

**(b) (ii) Prediction for $x = 8$ (3 marks):**

$$\hat{y} = 11.5238 + (6.1429)(8) = 11.5238 + 49.1429 = 60.6667$$

> **Predicted Monthly Salary** $\approx$ **₹60,667** for an employee with 8 years of experience.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a scatter plot with a suitable business example. Discuss the **three types of correlation patterns** that can be observed in a scatter plot with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The marketing department of a company collected the following data on monthly digital ad spend (₹ '000) and the corresponding website traffic (in thousands of visits):

| Month | Ad Spend $x_i$ | Traffic $y_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Jan | 10 | 50 |
| Feb | 12 | 55 |
| Mar | 15 | 65 |
| Apr | 18 | 70 |
| May | 20 | 80 |
| Jun | 22 | 85 |

**(i)** Compute the Pearson correlation coefficient and comment on the strength of the relationship. **(4 marks)**
**(ii)** Determine the regression equation of $Y$ on $X$ and estimate the traffic when ad spend is ₹30,000. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept, Example & Correlation Patterns (7 marks):**

A **scatter plot** is a bivariate graphical display where each data point $(x_i, y_i)$ represents one observation across two numerical variables. It is used to visually identify the relationship between variables before formal statistical modeling.

**Business Example:** An e-commerce company plots *discount percentage* (X-axis) against *units sold* (Y-axis) to determine if higher discounts actually drive more sales volume.

**Three Types of Correlation Patterns:**

1. **Positive Correlation** — Points trend upward from lower-left to upper-right. Example: Employee tenure vs. salary. Coefficient $r$ is between $0$ and $+1$.

2. **Negative Correlation** — Points trend downward from upper-left to lower-right. Example: Product price vs. units demanded. Coefficient $r$ is between $-1$ and $0$.

3. **No Correlation** — Points are randomly scattered with no discernible pattern. Example: Shoe size vs. intelligence quotient. Coefficient $r$ is approximately $0$.

*(Allocate ~2 marks for definition+example, ~3 marks for explaining the three patterns, ~2 marks for the conceptual understanding of $r$ values.)*

**(b) (i) Compute Pearson's $r$ (4 marks):**

With $n = 6$:

$$\sum x_i = 97, \quad \sum y_i = 405, \quad \sum x_i^2 = 1697, \quad \sum y_i^2 = 28375$$
$$\sum x_i y_i = (10)(50) + (12)(55) + (15)(65) + (18)(70) + (20)(80) + (22)(85)$$
$$= 500 + 660 + 975 + 1260 + 1600 + 1870 = 6865$$

Substituting:

$$r = \frac{6(6865) - (97)(405)}{\sqrt{[6(1697) - 9409][6(28375) - 164025]}}$$

$$r = \frac{41190 - 39285}{\sqrt{[10182 - 9409][170250 - 164025]}}$$

$$r = \frac{1905}{\sqrt{(773)(6225)}} = \frac{1905}{\sqrt{4811925}} = \frac{1905}{2193.61} \approx +0.8685$$

**Comment:** $r \approx +0.87$ indicates a **strong positive linear correlation** between ad spend and website traffic.

**(b) (ii) Regression Equation & Prediction (3 marks):**

Slope:

$$b = \frac{1905}{773} \approx 2.4644$$

Means: $\bar{x} = 97/6 \approx 16.1667$, $\bar{y} = 405/6 = 67.5$

Intercept:

$$a = 67.5 - (2.4644)(16.1667) = 67.5 - 39.8442 \approx 27.6558$$

**Regression equation:**

$$\hat{y} = 27.6558 + 2.4644\,x$$

**Prediction for $x = 30$:**

$$\hat{y} = 27.6558 + (2.4644)(30) = 27.6558 + 73.9319 = 101.5877$$

> **Estimated Website Traffic** $\approx$ **101,587 visits** when ad spend is ₹30,000.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unlabelled Axes (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently plot the points correctly but **forget to label axes with variable names and units**. This is the single most common deduction in Board valuation. Always write: *"X-axis: Ad Spend (₹ '000), Y-axis: Sales (₹ lakhs)"*.
> 2. **Confusing $r$ with $R^2$:** Reporting $r^2 = 0.97$ and then stating "97% of the data points fit the line" is **incorrect**. $R^2$ is the **proportion of variance explained**, not the proportion of points.
> 3. **Sign of $r$:** A negative slope in the data **must** produce a negative $r$. If the sign is missing or wrong, **deduct 1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the Interpretation:** Computing $r = 0.87$ without a concluding sentence (e.g., "strong positive correlation") loses **1 mark** at the Board level.
> 5. **Arithmetic in $r$:** Board examiners cross-verify $\sum x_i y_i$ and $\sum x_i^2$ independently. A single computational slip in the numerator/denominator **cascades** into wrong $r$, $b$, and $a$ — losing 4–5 marks cumulatively. Re-verify each sum twice.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam**

- A **scatter plot** visualizes the **bivariate relationship** between two numerical variables on a Cartesian plane. *(Module 3 core concept.)*
- Each point is plotted as $(x_i, y_i)$ where $X$ is the **independent** variable and $Y$ is the **dependent** variable.
- Always **label both axes with variable names AND units** — failure to do so costs marks.
- **Three correlation patterns:** Positive ($0 < r \le +1$), Negative ($-1 \le r < 0$), No correlation ($r \approx 0$).
- **Pearson $r$** is dimensionless and bounded: $-1 \le r \le +1$.
- **$R^2 = r^2$** — represents the **proportion of variance in $Y$** explained by $X$.
- **Regression line equation:** $\hat{y} = a + bx$, where $b = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\text{Var}(X)}$ and $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$.
- The **computational formula for $r$** is faster and less error-prone for raw data than the deviation formula.
- **$r = 0$ does NOT mean no relationship** — it only rules out *linear* association; non-linear patterns may still exist.
- **Outliers** can dramatically distort $r$ — always inspect the scatter plot before trusting the coefficient.
- **Use cases in business:** Marketing ROI, HR analytics, supply-demand, risk vs. return in finance, quality control.
- **Most common exam trap:** Forgetting units on axes, confusing $r$ with $R^2$, and missing the final interpretation sentence.
- For KTU Part A: focus on **definitions, types, and interpretation**. For Part B: expect **calculation of $r$, regression line, and prediction** from tabulated data.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coxcomb Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET611 - INTRODUCTION TO BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coxcomb Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Coxcomb Chart**, also known as a **Polar Area Diagram** or **Nightingale Rose Diagram**, is a circular statistical graphic in which quantitative data is encoded by sectors (wedges) of **equal angular width** but **variable radial length**. It was pioneered by **Florence Nightingale** in 1858 to communicate Crimean War mortality statistics to Queen Victoria and Members of Parliament.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *Coxcomb Chart* is a circular variation of the pie chart, invented by Florence Nightingale, in which each category occupies an equal angle (slice) of the circle, while the **radius of each slice is proportional to the value** of the category it represents. It is the foundational precursor of modern **Information Visualization**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Pizza Slice Growth" Model:**
> Imagine ordering a standard pizza that has been pre-cut into **8 perfectly equal slices**. In a normal pie chart, the *length of the outer crust* (arc length) would change based on the value. But in a Coxcomb Chart, every slice keeps the **same angle (45°)** at the center. Instead, to represent a bigger value, we **stretch the slice outward**, making the crust farther from the center. So a slice representing "Sales = 100" will have a longer radius than a slice representing "Sales = 50".
>
> Visualize the slices "blooming" outward like petals of a flower — hence the name *Coxcomb* (a rooster's crest flower).

### 1.3 Key Characteristics (Highlight Box)

> [!NOTE]
> **Salient Properties of a Coxcomb Chart**
> - All slices share an **identical central angle** ($\theta = \frac{360°}{n}$, where $n$ = number of categories).
> - The **radius ($r_i$)** of each slice is **directly proportional** to the value of the category.
> - The **area** of each slice is proportional to the **square** of the value (since $A \propto r^2$).
> - Particularly effective for displaying **cyclical / temporal data** (e.g., monthly data across a year).
> - Best suited for **positive values only** (negative radii are mathematically undefined).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Anatomy of the Chart

Let there be $n$ categories with data values $V_1, V_2, V_3, \dots, V_n$.

**Step 1 — Angular Allocation**
The total available angle around a circle is $2\pi$ radians ($360°$). Since all slices must be equal:

$$\theta = \frac{2\pi}{n} \quad \text{radians} = \frac{360°}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Radial Scaling**
A scaling constant $k$ is chosen (often the inverse of the maximum value, or determined by chart area):

$$r_i = k \cdot V_i$$

**Step 3 — Sector Area Calculation**
The area of sector $i$ is given by the standard polar area formula:

$$A_i = \frac{1}{2} \cdot r_i^{2} \cdot \theta = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (kV_i)^2 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{n} = \frac{\pi k^2}{n} \cdot V_i^2$$

This is the critical *why* — because radius is linear in $V$, but **area grows with $V^2$**, the eye perceives differences *more dramatically* than a simple bar chart.

### 2.2 Why "Equal Angle, Variable Radius"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Distinction vs. Pie Chart:**
> In a standard **pie chart**, the *angle* is proportional to value (and radius is constant). The eye perceives **area**.
> In a **Coxcomb chart**, the *angle* is constant and the **radius** is proportional to value. The eye still perceives **area**, but the perception is **quadratically amplified** by the radius scaling.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slice Angle | $\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{n}$ | radians (or $360°/n$ in degrees) |
| 2 | Radius of slice $i$ | $r_i = k \cdot V_i$ | $k$ is scaling constant |
| 3 | Area of slice $i$ | $A_i = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot r_i^{2} \cdot \theta$ | square units of chart canvas |
| 4 | Max Radius (capping rule) | $r_{max} = k \cdot V_{max}$ | largest slice determines chart size |
| 5 | Sum of slice areas | $\sum A_i = \dfrac{\pi k^{2}}{n} \sum V_i^{2}$ | not equal to total $\pi r^2$ (unlike pie) |
| 6 | Visual Scaling Factor | Area $\propto V_i^{2}$ | **quadratic perception** — important KTU fact |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Because area scales as $V_i^2$, a category with value **2× another** will appear **4× larger** to the eye. This is intentional (Nightingale's design for impact) but **must be disclosed** to readers for honest data ethics.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Business Analytics

- **Healthcare Analytics:** Nightingale's original use — tracking mortality causes over months.
- **Retail Analytics:** Comparing monthly sales of 12 product categories in one circular dashboard.
- **Operations Management:** Visualizing seasonal workforce demand (12 months around the circle).
- **Marketing Analytics:** Campaign performance across regions or channels, where each "petal" is a region.
- **Quality Control:** Defect distribution across 8 production lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Area Proportionality Proof

**Given:** A Coxcomb Chart with $n$ equal-angle slices, where the radius $r_i$ is proportional to the data value $V_i$.

**To Prove:** The perceived area of slice $i$ is proportional to $V_i^2$.

**Derivation:**

$$\text{Step 1: Standard formula for the area of a circular sector.}$$

$$A_i = \frac{1}{2} \cdot r_i^{2} \cdot \theta$$

**Step 2: Substitute the angular constraint (equal angles).**

$$\theta = \frac{2\pi}{n}$$

$$A_i = \frac{1}{2} \cdot r_i^{2} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{n} = \frac{\pi \cdot r_i^{2}}{n}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the radial constraint (radius proportional to value).**

$$r_i = k \cdot V_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_i^{2} = k^{2} \cdot V_i^{2}$$

$$A_i = \frac{\pi \cdot (k^{2} V_i^{2})}{n} = \frac{\pi k^{2}}{n} \cdot V_i^{2}$$

**Step 4: Since $\dfrac{\pi k^{2}}{n}$ is a constant for a given chart, we conclude:**

$$\boxed{A_i \propto V_i^{2}}$$

This is the *formal mathematical reason* why a Coxcomb Chart visually amplifies large values — a fact examiners frequently test. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A retail manager tracks quarterly sales (₹ Lakhs) for the year 2024. Construct a Coxcomb Chart analytically and verify the area proportionality.

**Data:**

| Quarter | Sales ($V_i$, ₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| Q1 | 10 |
| Q2 | 15 |
| Q3 | 20 |
| Q4 | 25 |

**Step 1 — Compute the equal slice angle.**

$$n = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac{360°}{4} = 90° \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Choose the scaling constant $k$.**

To fit the chart inside a unit circle, take $k = \dfrac{1}{V_{max}} = \dfrac{1}{25}$.

**Step 3 — Compute each slice radius.**

$$r_1 = \frac{1}{25} \cdot 10 = 0.40$$

$$r_2 = \frac{1}{25} \cdot 15 = 0.60$$

$$r_3 = \frac{1}{25} \cdot 20 = 0.80$$

$$r_4 = \frac{1}{25} \cdot 25 = 1.00$$

**Step 4 — Compute each sector area.**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.40)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.16}{4} = 0.0400\pi$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.60)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.36}{4} = 0.0900\pi$$

$$A_3 = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.80)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.64}{4} = 0.1600\pi$$

$$A_4 = \frac{\pi \cdot (1.00)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 1.00}{4} = 0.2500\pi$$

**Step 5 — Verify the $V_i^2$ proportionality.**

$$\frac{A_1}{V_1^{2}} = \frac{0.0400\pi}{100} = 4.00 \times 10^{-4}\pi$$

$$\frac{A_2}{V_2^{2}} = \frac{0.0900\pi}{225} = 4.00 \times 10^{-4}\pi$$

$$\frac{A_3}{V_3^{2}} = \frac{0.1600\pi}{400} = 4.00 \times 10^{-4}\pi$$

$$\frac{A_4}{V_4^{2}} = \frac{0.2500\pi}{625} = 4.00 \times 10^{-4}\pi$$

All four ratios are equal — confirming that **Area $\propto V_i^2$** under constant $k$ and $n$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Pie Chart | Bar Chart | **Coxcomb Chart** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Encoding Variable | Angle | Length (height) | **Radius** |
| Angle Usage | Variable | N/A (Cartesian) | **Fixed (= 360°/n)** |
| Visual Perception | Area $\propto V$ | Area $\propto V$ | **Area $\propto V^2$** |
| Best Use Case | Part-to-whole | Categorical comparison | **Cyclical / Temporal data** |
| Handles Negative Values? | No | Yes | **No** |
| Invented By | William Playfair (1801) | William Playfair (1786) | **Florence Nightingale (1858)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Coxcomb Chart Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Categorical Data V1 V2 ... Vn] --> B[Determine Number of Categories n]
    B --> C[Compute Equal Slice Angle theta = 360 / n]
    C --> D[Choose Scaling Constant k]
    D --> E[Compute Radius for Each Slice r_i = k x V_i]
    E --> F[Plot Sectors on Polar Coordinates]
    F --> G[Apply Color Coding by Category]
    G --> H[Add Labels and Legend]
    H --> I[Final Coxcomb Chart Output]
    I --> J{Interpretation Phase}
    J --> K[Linear Reading: Radius represents V_i]
    J --> L[Perceptual Reading: Area represents V_i squared]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Data Layer]
        D1[Category 1 Value]
        D2[Category 2 Value]
        D3[Category 3 Value]
        D4[Category N Value]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer[Mathematical Processing Layer]
        P1[Angular Allocator: theta equals 360 divided by N]
        P2[Radial Scaler: r equals k times V]
        P3[Area Calculator: A equals half r squared theta]
    end

    subgraph VisualizationLayer[Rendering Layer]
        R1[Polar Grid Canvas]
        R2[Equal Angle Sector Plotter]
        R3[Radial Vector Plotter]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        O1[Final Coxcomb Diagram]
        O2[Quadratic Area Perception]
    end

    D1 --> P1
    D2 --> P1
    D3 --> P1
    D4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P1 --> R1
    P2 --> R2
    P3 --> R3
    R1 --> O1
    R2 --> O1
    R3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
```

### 4.3 Schematic of a Single Slice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Center of Chart] -->|radius r_i| P[Outer Arc Point]
    O -->|angle theta| P
    S[Slice Sector Region] --> O
    S --> P
    A[Area A_i = 0.5 x r_i squared x theta] --> S
    V[Value V_i] --> R[Radius r_i = k x V_i]
    R --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define a Coxcomb Chart. Who invented it and in what year?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Coxcomb Chart is a circular statistical graphic in which **equal angular slices** are drawn, but the **radius of each slice is proportional to the data value** it represents. It is also called a **Polar Area Diagram**. It was invented by **Florence Nightingale** in the year **1858** while she was presenting mortality data from the Crimean War. The chart's unique feature is that its perceived area grows with the **square of the value**, making it visually impactful for communicating proportions in cyclical or temporal data. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
