**Step 1 — Compute promoters %.**

$$\%\text{Promoters} = \dfrac{110}{200} \times 100 = 55\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute detractors %.**

$$\%\text{Detractors} = \dfrac{30}{200} \times 100 = 15\%$$

**Step 3 — Subtract.**

$$\text{eNPS} = 55 - 15 = 40$$

**Interpretation:** An eNPS of **+40** is **world-class** (anything above +30 is considered excellent).

## 3.7 Derivation — Yield Ratio (Recruitment Funnel Metric)

### 3.7.1 Conceptual Derivation

Yield ratios track the percentage of candidates who progress from one recruitment stage to the next. A typical funnel:

$$\text{Applied} \to \text{Screened} \to \text{Interviewed} \to \text{Offered} \to \text{Hired}$$

For any stage transition:

$$\text{Yield Ratio}_n = \dfrac{\text{Candidates at stage } n}{\text{Candidates at stage } n-1} \times 100$$

### 3.7.2 Worked Example — Yield Ratios

**Problem:** Applications: 1,000; Screened: 400; Interviewed: 200; Offered: 80; Hired: 60. Compute each yield ratio.

**Step 1 — Screening yield.**

$$\text{YR}_{\text{scr}} = \dfrac{400}{1000} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**Step 2 — Interview yield.**

$$\text{YR}_{\text{int}} = \dfrac{200}{400} \times 100 = 50\%$$

**Step 3 — Offer yield.**

$$\text{YR}_{\text{off}} = \dfrac{80}{200} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**Step 4 — Acceptance yield (Offer Acceptance Rate).**

$$\text{YR}_{\text{acc}} = \dfrac{60}{80} \times 100 = 75\%$$

**Step 5 — Overall funnel yield.**

$$\text{YR}_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{60}{1000} \times 100 = 6\%$$

**Interpretation:** Only **6%** of applicants became hires — typical for high-volume tech hiring.

## 3.8 Master Analytical Matrix (Numerical Quick-Reference)

| Problem Pattern | Variables Identified | Formula Triggered | Numerical Shortcut |
|---|---|---|---|
| "How many left?" | Leavers, Avg HC | Turnover Rate | $\dfrac{L}{\text{Avg HC}} \times 100$ |
| "Days lost?" | Absent days, Working days, Avg HC | Absenteeism Rate | $\dfrac{\sum d}{W \times H} \times 100$ |
| "Cost of one hire?" | Total cost, Hires | CPH | $\dfrac{C}{n}$ |
| "Worth of training?" | Benefits, Costs | HR ROI | $\dfrac{B - C}{C} \times 100$ |
| "How productive?" | Revenue, Avg HC | RPE | $\dfrac{R}{H}$ |
| "How loyal?" | Promoters %, Detractors % | eNPS | $P - D$ |
| "Funnel strength?" | Stage counts | Yield Ratio | $\dfrac{n_k}{n_{k-1}} \times 100$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HR Analytics Decision Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The Mermaid block diagram below maps how raw HR data is transformed into strategic decisions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcA["HRIS System"] --> etl1["ETL and Data Cleaning Layer"]
    srcB["ATS Recruitment Platform"] --> etl1
    srcC["Engagement Survey Tool"] --> etl1
    srcD["Payroll and Compensation DB"] --> etl1
    etl1 --> dw["Centralized HR Data Warehouse"]
    dw --> d1["Descriptive Analytics Dashboards"]
    dw --> d2["Diagnostic Root Cause Engine"]
    dw --> d3["Predictive Attrition Model"]
    dw --> d4["Prescriptive Recommendation Engine"]
    d1 --> viz["Executive Visualization Layer"]
    d2 --> viz
    d3 --> viz
    d4 --> viz
    viz --> dec1["Talent Acquisition Decision"]
    viz --> dec2["Retention Intervention Decision"]
    viz --> dec3["Learning and Development Decision"]
    viz --> dec4["Compensation and Reward Decision"]
    dec1 --> mon["KPI Monitoring Loop"]
    dec2 --> mon
    dec3 --> mon
    dec4 --> mon
    mon --> etl1
```

## 4.2 Three-Tier HR Analytics Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tierA["Tier 1 Descriptive"] -->|"What happened"| kpi["Headcount Turnover Absenteeism"]
    tierB["Tier 2 Predictive"] -->|"What will happen"| ml["Attrition Model Performance Forecast"]
    tierC["Tier 3 Prescriptive"] -->|"What should we do"| opt["Optimal Pay Mix Retention Action"]
    tierA --> tierB
    tierB --> tierC
```

## 4.3 Recruitment Funnel with Yield Ratios

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stA["Applied 1000"] --> stB["Screened 400 YR 40 percent"]
    stB --> stC["Interviewed 200 YR 50 percent"]
    stC --> stD["Offered 80 YR 40 percent"]
    stD --> stE["Hired 60 Acceptance 75 percent"]
```

## 4.4 Kirkpatrick-Phillips Training Evaluation Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    l1["L1 Reaction Survey Score"] --> l2["L2 Learning Pre Test Post Test"]
    l2 --> l3["L3 Behaviour On Job Application"]
    l3 --> l4["L4 Results Business KPI Movement"]
    l4 --> l5["L5 ROI Financial Return"]
```

## 4.5 HR Analytics Maturity Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    m1["Stage 1 Operational Reports"] --> m2["Stage 2 Advanced Dashboards"]
    m2 --> m3["Stage 3 Strategic Predictive Models"]
    m3 --> m4["Stage 4 Prescriptive Optimization"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter in KTU 2024**: Examiner answer sheets reward *clear labelled flow diagrams*. Including a Mermaid-style schematic that you sketch on paper (boxes + arrows, with **metric names** written inside boxes) routinely fetches the **"presentation" component** of the 14-mark Part B question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define HR Analytics. Differentiate between Descriptive, Predictive, and Prescriptive HR Analytics. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** HR Analytics is the systematic use of people-data, statistical methods, and visualisation to measure, report, and improve workforce outcomes.

**Differences (2 marks):**

| Type | Question Answered | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Descriptive | *What happened?* | Monthly turnover dashboard |
| Predictive | *What is likely to happen?* | Logistic regression predicting 90-day attrition |
| Prescriptive | *What should we do?* | Optimisation engine recommending salary raise vs training |

---

### Question 2. Explain the Kirkpatrick model of training evaluation. Mention at least two levels. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Kirkpatrick Model** evaluates training programmes across **four levels** (Phillips adds a fifth — ROI):

1. **L1 — Reaction**: Trainees' perception of the programme; measured via feedback surveys.
2. **L2 — Learning**: Knowledge/skills gained; measured by pre-test vs post-test scores.
3. **L3 — Behaviour**: On-the-job application; measured by supervisor observation and 90-day KPI change.
4. **L4 — Results**: Business outcomes — e.g., sales uplift, defect reduction.
5. **L5 — ROI** *(Phillips)*: Financial return: $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Monetary Benefit}}{\text{Program Cost}} \times 100$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Full 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### (a) Define Turnover Rate and Absenteeism Rate. The opening headcount of a firm on 1 Jan 2024 was 1,200, closing headcount on 31 Dec 2024 was 800. 200 employees left during the year. The firm worked 240 days in 2024 with total absent days = 3,600. Compute Turnover Rate and Absenteeism Rate. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Turnover Rate (1 mark):** Turnover Rate is the percentage of employees who leave an organisation during a defined period, calculated on the average headcount.

**Definition of Absenteeism Rate (1 mark):** Absenteeism Rate is the percentage of scheduled working days lost due to employee absence.

**Turnover Calculation (2.5 marks):**

$$\text{Avg HC} = \dfrac{1200 + 800}{2} = 1000$$

$$\text{TR} = \dfrac{200}{1000} \times 100 = 20\%$$

**Absenteeism Calculation (2.5 marks):**

$$\text{AR} = \dfrac{3600}{240 \times 1000} \times 100 = \dfrac{3600}{240000} \times 100 = 1.5\%$$

**Interpretation:** 20% turnover is **high** (IT benchmark: 12–18%); 1.5% absenteeism is **healthy** (<3%).

#### (b) Explain the HR Analytics Lifecycle with a suitable real-world example. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **6-stage HR Analytics Lifecycle** is:

1. **Identify the Business Problem** — *e.g., Why is attrition 25% in the Bangalore sales team?*
2. **Data Collection** — HRIS records, engagement surveys, exit interviews, ATS data.
3. **Data Cleaning & Preparation** — Handle missing values, anonymise PII, standardise department codes.
4. **Analysis & Modelling** — Correlation analysis finds *"distance from office > 25 km"* and *"manager rating < 3"* as top attrition drivers. A logistic regression model then predicts 90-day attrition probability per employee.
5. **Visualisation & Storytelling** — Power BI dashboard segments employees into *High / Medium / Low* attrition risk with the top 3 drivers shown.
6. **Action & Monitoring** — Targeted interventions — flexible WFH, manager coaching, retention bonus. Monitor turnover monthly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students often list the lifecycle stages but forget to **link each stage back to the real-world example**. You will lose **2 marks** if the example is mentioned only at the start or end. Thread it through every stage.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — Full 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) A firm spent ₹12,00,000 on a leadership training programme. Productivity gains and error reductions generated ₹48,00,000 in monetised benefits. Calculate HR ROI. Explain the significance of HR ROI with examples of at least three HR interventions where ROI can be measured. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**HR ROI Computation (3 marks):**

$$\text{HR ROI} = \dfrac{4800000 - 1200000}{1200000} \times 100 = \dfrac{3600000}{1200000} \times 100 = 300\%$$

**Significance (1 mark):** HR ROI converts HR outcomes into financial language, enabling CFOs and CEOs to evaluate HR programmes on the same scale as any other business investment.

**Three Interventions (3 marks):**

1. **Recruitment Analytics** — ROI of an employer-branding campaign measured as: $\dfrac{\text{Saved Agency Fees} + \text{Reduced Vacancy Days}}{\text{Campaign Cost}} \times 100$.
2. **Training Analytics** — ROI of a sales-training programme measured by comparing pre- and post-training revenue per rep.
3. **Wellness Programmes** — ROI computed by comparing reduced health-insurance claims and absenteeism days against programme cost.

#### (b) Discuss the role of predictive analytics in employee attrition management. Mention any three techniques used. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Role (3 marks):** Predictive attrition analytics lets HR move from *reactive* exit interviews to *proactive* interventions. By scoring every employee with an attrition probability, HR can:
- Prioritise retention budget on high-impact, high-risk employees.
- Trigger manager alerts and stay-interviews automatically.
- Quantify financial exposure (replacement cost = 50–200% of annual salary).

**Three Techniques (4 marks):**

1. **Logistic Regression** — Outputs probability of attrition between 0 and 1; coefficients interpret as log-odds of each driver.
2. **Decision Trees / Random Forest** — Non-linear, easy to visualise; handles mixed categorical and numerical predictors well.
3. **Survival Analysis (Kaplan-Meier / Cox Proportional Hazards)** — Models *time-to-attrition*, ideal when HR wants to know "how many months until expected exit?".

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: For predictive analytics questions, students frequently write the technique names but forget to **state what each technique outputs**. Always end with a phrase like *"Logistic Regression outputs a probability score between 0 and 1."* This single sentence fetches the remaining 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Module 4: Human Resources**

### ✅ Definitions to Memorise
- **HR Analytics**, **People Analytics**, **Workforce Analytics** — synonyms.
- **Three Tiers**: Descriptive → Predictive → Prescriptive.
- **Diagnostic Analytics** — the often-missed "Why did it happen?" layer.

### ✅ Formulae to Memorise (High Yield)
- Turnover Rate $\rightarrow$ $\dfrac{L}{\text{Avg HC}} \times 100$
- Absenteeism Rate $\rightarrow$ $\dfrac{\sum d}{W \times H} \times 100$
- Cost per Hire $\rightarrow$ $\dfrac{C}{n}$
- HR ROI $\rightarrow$ $\dfrac{B - C}{C} \times 100$
- eNPS $\rightarrow$ $\%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$
- Revenue per Employee $\rightarrow$ $\dfrac{R}{\text{Avg HC}}$
- Yield Ratio $\rightarrow$ $\dfrac{n_k}{n_{k-1}} \times 100$

### ✅ Models to Memorise
- **Kirkpatrick-Phillips**: L1 Reaction, L2 Learning, L3 Behaviour, L4 Results, L5 ROI.
- **HR Analytics Maturity**: Operational → Advanced → Strategic → Prescriptive.
- **Recruitment Funnel**: Applied → Screened → Interviewed → Offered → Hired.

### ✅ Distinctions Examiners Love to Test
- **Time to Fill vs Time to Hire** — different start dates.
- **Internal vs External Recruitment Cost** components.
- **Promoter vs Passive vs Detractor** in eNPS.
- **Diagnostic vs Predictive Analytics**.

### ✅ Real-World Use Cases (Use in 14-mark answers)
- Attrition prediction at Infosys / TCS / Wipro.
- eNPS-driven engagement uplift at Google India.
- Training ROI measurement at Reliance Jio.

### ✅ Numerical Practice Priorities
1. Turnover Rate with opening/closing headcount.
2. Absenteeism Rate with multiple employees.
3. HR ROI with benefits vs costs.
4. eNPS with % Promoters and Detractors.
5. Yield Ratio across a 4-stage funnel.

> **Final KTU 2024 Tip**: In Part B, always combine **theory + numerical + diagram + real-world example** in a single answer. A 14-mark answer that includes all four components typically scores **12–14/14**, while an answer that includes only theory and numbers rarely crosses **9/14**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Financial Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET611 - INTRODUCTION TO BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Financial Analytics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — FINANCIAL ANALYTICS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Financial Analytics** is the discipline of applying quantitative methods, statistical models, computational algorithms, and data-driven techniques to evaluate the financial health, performance, and risk profile of an organization. It transforms raw financial data (transactions, statements, market data) into **actionable strategic insights** for investment, lending, budgeting, and valuation decisions.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, Financial Analytics is taught as a subdomain of **Business Analytics** that bridges:
- **Accounting** (historical record-keeping),
- **Finance** (capital and risk management),
- **Data Science** (predictive modelling and pattern recognition).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Doctor's Stethoscope on a Company**
> Think of a company as a human body. **Financial statements** are like the patient's medical report. **Financial Analytics** is the doctor (the analyst) who listens to the heartbeat using the stethoscope (ratios, models, KPIs) and prescribes a treatment plan (investment, cost-cutting, expansion, divestment). Just as a doctor needs both *symptoms* (numbers) and *medical knowledge* (models) to make a diagnosis, a financial analyst needs data **AND** analytical frameworks to convert figures into insight.

### 1.3 Core Components of Financial Analytics

| # | Component | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Financial Statement Analysis** | Examine Income Statement, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow |
| 2 | **Ratio Analysis** | Compute liquidity, profitability, solvency, efficiency ratios |
| 3 | **Time Value of Money (TVM)** | Discount future cash flows to present |
| 4 | **Capital Budgeting** | Evaluate long-term investment projects (NPV, IRR) |
| 5 | **Risk & Return Analytics** | Quantify uncertainty and expected payoff |
| 6 | **Forecasting** | Predict future revenue, expense, and cash flows |
| 7 | **Valuation Models** | Estimate intrinsic value of firm / asset |
| 8 | **Portfolio Analytics** | Optimise asset mix for risk-adjusted return |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 focuses primarily on **Ratio Analysis, Time Value of Money, Capital Budgeting (NPV/IRR/Payback), Risk-Return trade-off, and Forecasting** techniques applied to financial data.

### 1.4 Why Financial Analytics Matters

- **Investment Decisions:** Banks, mutual funds, and PE firms use it before committing capital.
- **Operational Efficiency:** CFOs use it to spot cost leaks and margin pressure.
- **Strategic Planning:** Forecasts drive 3–5 year strategic roadmaps.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** SEBI, RBI, IFRS, and GAAP mandates require rigorous financial reporting analytics.
- **Risk Mitigation:** Stress-testing, scenario analysis, and VaR protect against bankruptcy.

### 1.5 The Analytics Maturity Ladder

> [!NOTE]
> **Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive**
> Financial Analytics follows the same maturity ladder as general analytics:
> 1. **Descriptive:** "What happened?" (e.g., revenue fell 12% QoQ)
> 2. **Diagnostic:** "Why did it happen?" (e.g., raw material cost up 18%)
> 3. **Predictive:** "What will happen?" (e.g., next quarter forecast = -5% margin)
> 4. **Prescriptive:** "What should we do?" (e.g., hedge currency, renegotiate supplier contracts)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Financial Statements

### A. Income Statement (Profit & Loss Account)
Reports **revenues**, **expenses**, and **profit/loss** over a period.

$$\text{Net Profit} = \text{Revenue} - \text{COGS} - \text{Operating Expenses} - \text{Interest} - \text{Taxes}$$

### B. Balance Sheet
Snapshot of **Assets, Liabilities, and Equity** at a point in time.

$$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Shareholders' Equity}$$

### C. Cash Flow Statement
Tracks **operating, investing, and financing** cash movements.

$$\text{Net Cash Flow} = \text{Cash from Operations} + \text{Cash from Investing} + \text{Cash from Financing}$$

---

## 2.2 Financial Ratio Analysis

### A. Liquidity Ratios (Short-term solvency)

$$\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}} \quad ; \quad \text{Ideal} \geq 2.0$$

$$\text{Quick Ratio} = \frac{\text{Current Assets} - \text{Inventory}}{\text{Current Liabilities}} \quad ; \quad \text{Ideal} \geq 1.0$$

$$\text{Cash Ratio} = \frac{\text{Cash} + \text{Marketable Securities}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$$

### B. Profitability Ratios

$$\text{Gross Profit Margin} = \frac{\text{Revenue} - \text{COGS}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Net Profit Margin} = \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Return on Assets (ROA)} = \frac{\text{Net Income}}{\text{Total Assets}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Return on Equity (ROE)} = \frac{\text{Net Income}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}} \times 100\%$$

### C. Solvency / Leverage Ratios

$$\text{Debt-to-Equity} = \frac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}}$$

$$\text{Interest Coverage Ratio} = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Interest Expense}}$$

$$\text{Debt Ratio} = \frac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Assets}}$$

### D. Efficiency / Activity Ratios

$$\text{Inventory Turnover} = \frac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$$

$$\text{Accounts Receivable Turnover} = \frac{\text{Net Credit Sales}}{\text{Average Accounts Receivable}}$$

$$\text{Asset Turnover} = \frac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Average Total Assets}}$$

---

## 2.3 Time Value of Money (TVM) — The Heart of Financial Analytics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** *A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow* because today's rupee can be invested to earn interest and because of inflation erosion.

### A. Future Value (FV) of a Lump Sum

$$FV = PV \times (1 + r)^n$$

Where:
- $PV$ = Present Value
- $r$ = Rate of interest per period (decimal)
- $n$ = Number of periods
- $(1+r)^n$ = **Future Value Interest Factor (FVIF)**

### B. Present Value (PV) of a Lump Sum

$$PV = \frac{FV}{(1 + r)^n}$$

$(1+r)^{-n}$ = **Present Value Interest Factor (PVIF)**

### C. Future Value of an Annuity (Ordinary Annuity — payments at period END)

$$FV_{\text{annuity}} = PMT \times \frac{(1 + r)^n - 1}{r}$$

### D. Present Value of an Annuity

$$PV_{\text{annuity}} = PMT \times \frac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r}$$

### E. Perpetuity (Infinite cash flows)

$$PV_{\text{perpetuity}} = \frac{PMT}{r}$$

### F. Growing Perpetuity

$$PV = \frac{PMT_1}{r - g} \quad ; \quad r > g$$

---

## 2.4 Capital Budgeting Techniques

### A. Net Present Value (NPV)

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - CF_0$$

> **Decision Rule:** $NPV > 0 \Rightarrow$ **Accept** | $NPV < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Reject**

### B. Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

The discount rate $r^*$ at which NPV = 0:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + r^*)^t} = 0$$

> **Decision Rule:** $IRR > \text{Cost of Capital} \Rightarrow$ **Accept**

### C. Profitability Index (PI)

$$PI = \frac{PV \text{ of future cash flows}}{\text{Initial Investment}} = 1 + \frac{NPV}{\text{Initial Investment}}$$

> **Decision Rule:** $PI > 1 \Rightarrow$ **Accept**

### D. Payback Period

$$\text{Payback Period} = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$$

### E. Discounted Payback Period
The number of years it takes for cumulative **discounted** cash flows to equal the initial investment.

---

## 2.5 Risk & Return Metrics

### A. Expected Return (Probability-weighted)

$$E(R) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times R_i$$

### B. Variance & Standard Deviation (Total Risk)

$$\sigma^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times [R_i - E(R)]^2 \quad ; \quad \sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$$

### C. Coefficient of Variation (Relative Risk)

$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{E(R)}$$

### D. Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \times \left[E(R_m) - R_f\right]$$

Where:
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate
- $\beta_i$ = Systematic risk of asset $i$
- $E(R_m)$ = Expected market return
- $E(R_m) - R_f$ = Market risk premium

### E. Sharpe Ratio (Risk-adjusted return per unit of TOTAL risk)

$$\text{Sharpe Ratio} = \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p}$$

### F. Value at Risk (VaR)
The maximum loss over a time horizon at a given confidence level (e.g., 95% VaR = ₹50 L means 5% chance of losing > ₹50 L).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for KTU exams:** Memorise the formula, the **decision rule**, and the **assumption** (e.g., NPV assumes reinvestment at cost of capital; IRR assumes reinvestment at IRR itself).

| # | Concept | Formula | Decision Rule / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Future Value (lump sum) | $FV = PV(1+r)^n$ | Compounding |
| 2 | Present Value (lump sum) | $PV = FV(1+r)^{-n}$ | Discounting |
| 3 | PV of Annuity | $PV = PMT \cdot \frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | Ordinary annuity |
| 4 | FV of Annuity | $FV = PMT \cdot \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r}$ | Ordinary annuity |
| 5 | Perpetuity | $PV = PMT/r$ | Constant cash flow forever |
| 6 | NPV | $\sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Accept if NPV > 0 |
| 7 | IRR | NPV = 0 | Accept if IRR > cost of capital |
| 8 | PI | PV of inflows / Initial investment | Accept if PI > 1 |
| 9 | Payback | $I_0 / \text{Annual CF}$ | Accept if payback < target |
| 10 | Current Ratio | CA / CL | Healthy ≥ 2.0 |
| 11 | Quick Ratio | (CA − Inv) / CL | Healthy ≥ 1.0 |
| 12 | ROE | NI / Equity × 100 | Higher = better |
| 13 | ROA | NI / Assets × 100 | Higher = better |
| 14 | D/E Ratio | Debt / Equity | Lower = safer |
| 15 | Expected Return | $\sum P_i R_i$ | Probability weighted |
| 16 | Std Deviation | $\sqrt{\sum P_i(R_i-E(R))^2}$ | Total risk |
| 17 | CAPM | $R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | Cost of equity |
| 18 | Sharpe Ratio | $(R_p - R_f) / \sigma_p$ | Higher = better |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Manufacturing Firms:** Use ratio analysis to decide whether to take a loan for plant expansion.
- **Startups:** Use NPV to evaluate R&D projects; use runway analytics (cash-burn forecasting).
- **Banks & NBFCs:** Use CAPM to price risk in loan portfolios; use VaR for capital adequacy.
- **Investment Firms:** Use Sharpe Ratio, Beta, and CAPM to construct optimal portfolios.
- **Consultancies:** Use ratio benchmarking to advise clients on industry-specific performance gaps.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Time Value of Money (Lump Sum)

**Problem:** A sum of ₹1,00,000 is invested today at 10% per annum for 5 years. Compute the future value.

### Step 1 — Identify Variables
- $PV = 1,00,000$
- $r = 0.10$
- $n = 5$

### Step 2 — Apply Formula

$$FV = PV \times (1 + r)^n$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$FV = 1{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + 0.10)^5$$

### Step 4 — Compute Compound Factor

$$(1.10)^5 = 1.10 \times 1.10 \times 1.10 \times 1.10 \times 1.10$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^2 &= 1.21 \\
(1.10)^3 &= 1.21 \times 1.10 = 1.331 \\
(1.10)^4 &= 1.331 \times 1.10 = 1.4641 \\
(1.10)^5 &= 1.4641 \times 1.10 = 1.61051
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Final Answer

$$FV = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 1.61051 = \text{₹ } 1{,}61{,}051$$

> **Interpretation:** Money grew by **₹61,051** (≈61%) over 5 years purely due to compounding at 10%.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — PV of an Annuity

**Problem:** A project yields ₹25,000 every year-end for 6 years. Discount rate is 12% p.a. Find PV.

### Step 1 — Variables
- $PMT = 25{,}000$
- $r = 0.12$
- $n = 6$

### Step 2 — Formula

$$PV_{\text{annuity}} = PMT \times \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

### Step 3 — Discount Factor

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^6 &= 1.12 \times 1.12 \times 1.12 \times 1.12 \times 1.12 \times 1.12 \\
(1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \\
(1.12)^3 &= 1.2544 \times 1.12 = 1.4049 \\
(1.12)^6 &= (1.4049)^2 = 1.9738
\end{aligned}$$

$$(1.12)^{-6} = \frac{1}{1.9738} = 0.5066$$

### Step 4 — Annuity Factor

$$\frac{1 - 0.5066}{0.12} = \frac{0.4934}{0.12} = 4.1114$$

### Step 5 — Final PV

$$PV = 25{,}000 \times 4.1114 = \text{₹ } 1{,}02{,}785$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — NPV Computation

**Problem:** Initial investment ₹5,00,000. Cash inflows: Year 1 = ₹1,50,000; Year 2 = ₹2,00,000; Year 3 = ₹2,50,000. Discount rate = 10%.

### Step 1 — Discount Each Cash Flow

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{(1.10)^1} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{1.10} = 1{,}36{,}364 \\
PV_2 &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^2} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.21} = 1{,}65{,}289 \\
PV_3 &= \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{(1.10)^3} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.331} = 1{,}87{,}828
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Sum of PVs of Inflows

$$\sum PV = 1{,}36{,}364 + 1{,}65{,}289 + 1{,}87{,}828 = 4{,}89{,}481$$

### Step 3 — NPV

$$NPV = 4{,}89{,}481 - 5{,}00{,}000 = -\text{₹ }10{,}519$$

### Step 4 — Decision

> [!WARNING]
> NPV is **negative (-₹10,519)** → **REJECT** the project. Even though total undiscounted inflows (₹6,00,000) exceed investment, the time value of money makes it unattractive.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — IRR by Interpolation

**Problem:** Initial investment = ₹1,00,000. Cash inflows: Y1 = ₹40,000, Y2 = ₹50,000, Y3 = ₹60,000. Find IRR.

### Step 1 — Set NPV Equation to Zero

$$\frac{40{,}000}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{50{,}000}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{60{,}000}{(1+r)^3} - 1{,}00{,}000 = 0$$

### Step 2 — Trial at r = 20%

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 40{,}000 / 1.20 = 33{,}333 \\
PV_2 &= 50{,}000 / 1.44 = 34{,}722 \\
PV_3 &= 60{,}000 / 1.728 = 34{,}722 \\
\text{Sum} &= 1{,}02{,}777 \\
NPV &= 1{,}02{,}777 - 1{,}00{,}000 = +2{,}777
\end{aligned}$$

NPV at 20% is **positive** → IRR > 20%.

### Step 3 — Trial at r = 25%

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 40{,}000 / 1.25 = 32{,}000 \\
PV_2 &= 50{,}000 / 1.5625 = 32{,}000 \\
PV_3 &= 60{,}000 / 1.953125 = 30{,}720 \\
\text{Sum} &= 94{,}720 \\
NPV &= 94{,}720 - 1{,}00{,}000 = -5{,}280
\end{aligned}$$

NPV at 25% is **negative** → IRR < 25%.

### Step 4 — Linear Interpolation

$$IRR \approx r_L + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (r_H - r_L)$$

$$IRR = 20 + \frac{2777}{2777 - (-5280)} \times (25 - 20)$$

$$IRR = 20 + \frac{2777}{8057} \times 5 = 20 + 1.72 \approx 21.72\%$$

> **Decision:** If cost of capital < 21.72%, **Accept**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Ratio Analysis (Full Set)

**Given Data (₹ in Lakhs):**

| Item | Amount |
|---|---|
| Sales | 800 |
| COGS | 500 |
| Net Profit | 80 |
| Current Assets | 300 |
| Inventory | 100 |
| Current Liabilities | 150 |
| Total Assets | 1000 |
| Equity | 600 |
| Debt | 400 |
| EBIT | 150 |

### Computed Ratios

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Ratio} &= \frac{300}{150} = 2.0 \\
\text{Quick Ratio} &= \frac{300 - 100}{150} = 1.33 \\
\text{Gross Margin} &= \frac{800-500}{800} \times 100 = 37.5\% \\
\text{Net Margin} &= \frac{80}{800} \times 100 = 10\% \\
\text{ROA} &= \frac{80}{1000} \times 100 = 8\% \\
\text{ROE} &= \frac{80}{600} \times 100 = 13.33\% \\
\text{D/E} &= \frac{400}{600} = 0.67 \\
\text{Interest Coverage} &= \frac{150}{\text{Interest}} \\
\text{Asset Turnover} &= \frac{800}{1000} = 0.8
\end{aligned}$$

### Interpretation
- Current Ratio = 2.0 → Healthy short-term liquidity.
- ROE > ROA → Efficient use of financial leverage.
- D/E = 0.67 → Moderate leverage; financially stable.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — CAPM Application

**Problem:** Risk-free rate = 6%, Market return = 14%, Stock Beta = 1.5. Find expected return.

$$E(R) = R_f + \beta \times [E(R_m) - R_f]$$

$$E(R) = 6 + 1.5 \times (14 - 6) = 6 + 1.5 \times 8 = 6 + 12 = 18\%$$

> **Interpretation:** Because beta > 1, this stock amplifies market movements; it must yield 18% to compensate for the higher systematic risk.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example 7 — Expected Return and Standard Deviation

**Stock A returns under three scenarios:**

| Scenario | Probability | Return |
|---|---|---|
| Boom | 0.3 | 25% |
| Normal | 0.5 | 15% |
| Recession | 0.2 | -5% |

### Expected Return

$$E(R) = (0.3)(25) + (0.5)(15) + (0.2)(-5) = 7.5 + 7.5 - 1.0 = 14\%$$

### Variance

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= 0.3 \times (25 - 14)^2 + 0.5 \times (15 - 14)^2 + 0.2 \times (-5 - 14)^2 \\
&= 0.3 \times 121 + 0.5 \times 1 + 0.2 \times 361 \\
&= 36.3 + 0.5 + 72.2 = 109.0
\end{aligned}$$

### Standard Deviation

$$\sigma = \sqrt{109.0} \approx 10.44\%$$

### Coefficient of Variation

$$CV = \frac{10.44}{14} = 0.746$$

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What?

| Decision Context | Best Tool | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Mutually exclusive projects of unequal size | **NPV** | NPV measures absolute wealth addition |
| Quick screening of many small projects | **PI** | Comparable across investment sizes |
| Communicate project yield in % | **IRR** | Intuitive percentage return |
| Highly uncertain / weak data | **Payback** | Conservative, risk-averse |
| Portfolio construction | **Sharpe / CAPM** | Risk-adjusted comparisons |
| Loan default prediction | **Credit Scoring (Z-score)** | Combines multiple ratios |
| Daily trading risk limit | **VaR** | Quantifies tail loss |

---

## 3.9 DuPont Identity (ROE Decomposition)

$$\text{ROE} = \underbrace{\frac{\text{Net Income}}{\text{Sales}}}_{\text{Net Margin}} \times \underbrace{\frac{\text{Sales}}{\text{Total Assets}}}_{\text{Asset Turnover}} \times \underbrace{\frac{\text{Total Assets}}{\text{Equity}}}_{\text{Equity Multiplier (Leverage)}}$$

> **Why important:** Shows whether ROE is driven by profitability, efficiency, or leverage — critical for diagnosing strategy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Financial Analytics Pipeline

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Define Financial Analytics and list its four key components. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
Financial Analytics is the application of quantitative, statistical, and computational techniques to financial data for the purpose of evaluating performance, risk, and valuation.

**Four Key Components:**
1. **Financial Statement Analysis** — interpretation of income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow.
2. **Ratio Analysis** — computation of liquidity, profitability, solvency, and efficiency ratios.
3. **Capital Budgeting** — NPV, IRR, PI, Payback Period.
4. **Risk and Return Analytics** — standard deviation, beta, CAPM, Sharpe ratio.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for four components = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 — Differentiate between NPV and IRR. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | NPV | IRR |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Absolute ₹ value added | Discount rate at which NPV = 0 |
| Output | In currency units | In percentage |
| Decision Rule | Accept if NPV > 0 | Accept if IRR > Cost of Capital |
| Reinvestment Assumption | At cost of capital | At IRR itself |
| Mutually Exclusive Projects | Reliable | May give conflicting ranking |
| Scale | Considers project size | Ignores project size |

*(3 marks for tabular comparison with at least 4 valid points)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**A company is evaluating a project requiring an initial investment of ₹10,00,000. The expected cash inflows are:**

| Year | Cash Inflow (₹) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 3,00,000 |
| 2 | 4,00,000 |
| 3 | 5,00,000 |
| 4 | 3,00,000 |

**The cost of capital is 12% p.a.**

### Part (a) — Compute the NPV and PI of the project. Advise whether to accept it. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Discount factors at 12%**

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^1 &= 1.1200 \Rightarrow DF_1 = 0.8929 \\
(1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \Rightarrow DF_2 = 0.7972 \\
(1.12)^3 &= 1.4049 \Rightarrow DF_3 = 0.7118 \\
(1.12)^4 &= 1.5735 \Rightarrow DF_4 = 0.6355
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — PV of each cash inflow**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8929 = 2{,}67{,}857 \\
PV_2 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7972 = 3{,}18{,}878 \\
PV_3 &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7118 = 3{,}55{,}900 \\
PV_4 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6355 = 1{,}90{,}650 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total PV of inflows**

$$\sum PV = 2{,}67{,}857 + 3{,}18{,}878 + 3{,}55{,}900 + 1{,}90{,}650 = 11{,}33{,}285$$

**Step 4 — NPV**

$$NPV = 11{,}33{,}285 - 10{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}33{,}285$$

**Step 5 — PI**

$$PI = \frac{11{,}33{,}285}{10{,}00{,}000} = 1.133$$

**Decision:** NPV > 0 and PI > 1 → **ACCEPT the project.** It adds ₹1,33,285 in present value terms.

---

### Part (b) — Compute the IRR using interpolation method. Comment on the result. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Try r = 18%**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 3{,}00{,}000 / 1.18 = 2{,}54{,}237 \\
PV_2 &= 4{,}00{,}000 / 1.3924 = 2{,}87{,}273 \\
PV_3 &= 5{,}00{,}000 / 1.6430 = 3{,}04{,}323 \\
PV_4 &= 3{,}00{,}000 / 1.9388 = 1{,}54{,}735 \\
\text{Sum} &= 10{,}00{,}568 \\
NPV_{18\%} &= +568
\end{aligned}$$

NPV is **slightly positive** at 18% → IRR is just above 18%.

**Step 2 — Try r = 19%**

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.19)^1 &= 1.19 \\
(1.19)^2 &= 1.4161 \\
(1.19)^3 &= 1.6852 \\
(1.19)^4 &= 2.0053 \\
PV_1 &= 3{,}00{,}000 / 1.19 = 2{,}52{,}101 \\
PV_2 &= 4{,}00{,}000 / 1.4161 = 2{,}82{,}464 \\
PV_3 &= 5{,}00{,}000 / 1.6852 = 2{,}96{,}700 \\
PV_4 &= 3{,}00{,}000 / 2.0053 = 1{,}49{,}605 \\
\text{Sum} &= 9{,}80{,}870 \\
NPV_{19\%} &= -19{,}130
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpolation**

$$IRR = 18 + \frac{568}{568 - (-19130)} \times (19 - 18)$$

$$IRR = 18 + \frac{568}{19698} \times 1 = 18 + 0.0288 \approx 18.03\%$$

**Comment:** Since IRR (18.03%) > Cost of Capital (12%), the project is **acceptable** and consistent with NPV decision.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**From the following data of XYZ Ltd, compute: (a) Current Ratio, Quick Ratio, Debt-Equity Ratio, Inventory Turnover Ratio, ROE, ROA, and Net Profit Margin (7 marks). (b) Analyse the financial health of the company and provide recommendations (7 marks).**

| Item | ₹ in Lakhs |
|---|---|
| Sales | 1200 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 800 |
| Net Profit | 120 |
| Current Assets | 500 |
| Inventory | 200 |
| Current Liabilities | 250 |
| Total Assets | 1500 |
| Shareholders' Equity | 800 |
| Total Debt | 700 |

### Part (a) — Ratio Computation (7 Marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Ratio} &= \frac{500}{250} = 2.0 \\
\text{Quick Ratio} &= \frac{500 - 200}{250} = \frac{300}{250} = 1.2 \\
\text{Debt-Equity Ratio} &= \frac{700}{800} = 0.875 \\
\text{Inventory Turnover} &= \frac{800}{200} = 4 \text{ times} \\
\text{ROE} &= \frac{120}{800} \times 100 = 15\% \\
\text{ROA} &= \frac{120}{1500} \times 100 = 8\% \\
\text{Net Profit Margin} &= \frac{120}{1200} \times 100 = 10\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (b) — Financial Health Analysis & Recommendations (7 Marks)

**Liquidity Analysis:**
- Current Ratio = 2.0 → healthy (industry norm ≥ 2).
- Quick Ratio = 1.2 → comfortable; the firm can meet short-term obligations even without selling inventory.

**Solvency Analysis:**
- D/E = 0.875 → moderately leveraged; acceptable but on the higher side.
- The company has ₹0.875 of debt for every ₹1 of equity — manageable.

**Profitability:**
- Net Margin = 10% → reasonable.
- ROE (15%) > ROA (8%) → positive financial leverage; equity holders are benefitting.

**Efficiency:**
- Inventory Turnover = 4 → inventory is turned over 4 times a year (~91 days of inventory held). Could be improved.

**Recommendations:**
1. Reduce inventory holding period through **just-in-time (JIT)** procurement.
2. Consider repaying high-cost debt to improve interest coverage.
3. Improve asset utilisation to push ROA higher.
4. Maintain strong liquidity buffer for resilience.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Financial Analytics Questions:**
> 1. **Discount factor precision:** Round-off errors in $(1+r)^n$ lead to wrong NPV. Always show **5 decimal places** for the DF.
> 2. **Annuity vs. Annuity Due confusion:** Ordinary annuity = end of period; Annuity Due = beginning. Different factors.
> 3. **IRR Interpolation sign error:** Use the formula $IRR = r_L + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (r_H - r_L)$. The denominator is the **difference** (not sum).
> 4. **NPV decision rule:** Many students wrongly reject if NPV is "small positive." Any positive NPV means wealth creation — accept it.
> 5. **Ratio interpretation:** Quoting a number without industry benchmark earns **partial** marks only. Always add a one-line interpretation.
> 6. **CAPM formula:** Writing "CAPM = R_f + Beta" is incomplete. The full form is $R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$.
> 7. **Cash flow vs. profit:** NPV uses **cash flows**, NOT accounting profits. Depreciation must be added back if using profit figures.
> 8. **Payback ignores TVM:** Mention this as a limitation to score full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Module 4: Financial Analytics**

- [x] Financial Analytics = data + finance + analytics → insight.
- [x] **Three statements:** Income Statement, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow Statement.
- [x] **Accounting equation:** Assets = Liabilities + Equity.
- [x] **Liquidity ratios:** Current Ratio, Quick Ratio, Cash Ratio. Healthy: Current ≥ 2, Quick ≥ 1.
- [x] **Profitability ratios:** Gross Margin, Net Margin, ROA, ROE.
- [x] **Leverage ratios:** D/E, Interest Coverage, Debt Ratio.
- [x] **Efficiency ratios:** Inventory Turnover, Receivable Turnover, Asset Turnover.
- [x] **TVM — Core formulas:** $FV = PV(1+r)^n$, $PV = FV(1+r)^{-n}$.
- [x] **Annuity formulas:** Know ordinary annuity (end) vs annuity due (begin).
- [x] **Perpetuity:** $PV = PMT/r$ (constant cash flows forever).
- [x] **NPV Decision Rule:** Accept if NPV > 0.
- [x] **IRR Decision Rule:** Accept if IRR > Cost of Capital.
- [x] **PI Decision Rule:** Accept if PI > 1.
- [x] **Payback:** Simple = undiscounted; Discounted = accounts for TVM.
- [x] **CAPM:** $R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$.
- [x] **Sharpe Ratio:** $(R_p - R_f) / \sigma_p$ — higher is better.
- [x] **Expected Return:** Probability-weighted average of returns.
- [x] **Standard Deviation:** Measures total risk (systematic + unsystematic).
- [x] **Beta:** Measures only systematic (market) risk.
- [x] **VaR:** Maximum expected loss at a confidence level.
- [x] **DuPont Identity:** ROE = Net Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier.
- [x] **Forecasting tools:** Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing, Regression, ARIMA.
- [x] **Reinvestment assumption:** NPV assumes at cost of capital; IRR assumes at IRR.
- [x] **Mutually exclusive projects:** Use NPV, NOT IRR (because of conflicting rankings).
- [x] **Show 5-decimal discount factors** in NPV calculations to avoid valuation errors.

---

**End of Module 4 — Financial Analytics Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production and Operations Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET611 - INTRODUCTION TO BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Production and Operations Analytics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Production and Operations Analytics (POA)** is the discipline of using **quantitative methods, statistical models, optimization techniques, and data-driven algorithms** to measure, analyze, forecast, and improve the efficiency of an organization's transformation processes (converting inputs into goods and services).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEMET611)** syllabus, Production and Operations Analytics encompasses the application of analytical tools to:

- **Plan** production schedules and capacity utilization
- **Forecast** future demand using historical data
- **Optimize** inventory levels, supply chain flows, and resource allocation
- **Control** quality through statistical process control
- **Measure** productivity and operational efficiency

### Core Components of the Production System

| Element | Function | Example Metric |
|---------|----------|----------------|
| **Input** | Resources consumed | Raw materials, labor hours, capital |
| **Transformation Process** | Conversion mechanism | Assembly line, service delivery |
| **Output** | Final product/service | Units produced, customers served |
| **Feedback** | Quality & control loop | Defect rates, customer complaints |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Kitchen" 🍳

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you run a busy restaurant kitchen (the production system).** Every morning you must decide:
> 1. How much rice to cook (inventory decision)?
> 2. How many waiters to schedule based on predicted lunch crowd (forecasting)?
> 3. How to ensure every dish tastes the same (quality control)?
> 4. How to maximize tables served per hour (productivity)?

**Production and Operations Analytics is the "smart kitchen manager"** — it uses data (yesterday's customer count, seasonal patterns, ingredient costs) to make optimal decisions automatically. Instead of the head chef *guessing* how many biryanis to prepare, analytics provides a **mathematical answer** based on demand patterns, holding costs, and ordering costs.

### Why It Matters in B.Tech Engineering

For an engineer in Kerala working in **manufacturing, IT services, or logistics**, POA skills are essential because:
- **Kerala's industrial landscape** (Kerala State Electronics Development Corporation, Cochin Shipyard, Infosys campuses) increasingly relies on **Industry 4.0** principles
- **NEP 2020** emphasizes outcome-based learning where engineers must solve *real industrial problems* using data
- **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (COs)** for this module target the ability to *apply forecasting, optimization, and statistical control* to operational scenarios

### Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) — The "Vital Signs" of Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three universal KPIs in Production Analytics are:
> 1. **Productivity** = Output / Input
> 2. **Efficiency** = Standard Output / Actual Input × 100%
> 3. **Utilization** = Actual Capacity Used / Maximum Capacity × 100%

For example, a CNC machine that produces **80 units in 8 hours** when its standard is **100 units** has:
- **Productivity** = 80/8 = **10 units/hour**
- **Efficiency** = (100/80) × 100% = **125%** (operator is over-performing standard)
- **Utilization** = depends on shift availability
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Production Analytics

### Pillar 1: Demand Forecasting 📈

Forecasting is the **backbone** of production planning. It answers the question: *"How much will our customers want tomorrow?"*

**Why forecasting matters:**
- Reduces **stockouts** (lost sales) and **overstocking** (waste, holding cost)
- Drives **procurement**, **staffing**, and **production scheduling** decisions
- Foundation for **EOQ**, **MRP**, and **JIT** systems

**Two major families of forecasting methods:**

| Method | Type | Best Used For | Complexity |
|--------|------|---------------|------------|
| **Naive Forecast** | Qualitative | Stable demand | Very Low |
| **Simple Moving Average (SMA)** | Quantitative | Removing random noise | Low |
| **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** | Quantitative | Recent data more important | Medium |
| **Exponential Smoothing (ES)** | Quantitative | Trendless series | Medium |
| **Trend-Adjusted ES (Holt's Method)** | Quantitative | Series with trends | High |

---

### Pillar 2: Inventory Analytics 📦

> [!NOTE]
> **Inventory** is the stock of raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods held by an organization. The central problem is the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) trade-off**:

Every time you **order**, you pay an **ordering cost (S)**. Every time you **hold** items, you pay a **holding cost (H)**. The EOQ model finds the **optimal order size that minimizes the TOTAL of these two costs**.

```
        Total Cost Curve (U-Shape)
              |
        TC    |    * <-- Minimum Total Cost at EOQ
              |   * *
              |  *   *
              | *     *
              |*-------*---  Ordering Cost (decreases as Q increases)
              *--------*--  Holding Cost (increases as Q increases)
              +-----------------> Q (Order Quantity)
                    ↑
                  EOQ*
```

**Assumptions of the Basic EOQ Model (Harris-Wilson Model, 1913):**
1. Demand rate is **constant and known** (D units/year)
2. Lead time is **fixed and known**
3. Order is received **instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate)
4. **No stockouts allowed**
5. Holding cost is **linear** in inventory level

### Pillar 3: Quality Analytics & Statistical Process Control (SPC) 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Analytics** uses statistics to distinguish between two types of variation in a process:
> - **Common Cause Variation** (inherent, random) — within control limits
> - **Special Cause Variation** (assignable, abnormal) — indicates a problem

The primary tool is the **Control Chart**, invented by **Walter Shewhart (1924)** at Bell Labs.

**Six Sigma Quality Level:**
- A process operating at **±6 standard deviations** from the mean produces only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**
- This is the gold standard in modern manufacturing (used by Motorola, GE, Toyota)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear every year in KTU ESE papers!**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|------|
| 1 | Simple Moving Average | $F_{t+1} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=t-n+1}^{t} A_i$ | $A_i$=actual, $n$=periods | units |
| 2 | Weighted Moving Average | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i A_{t-i+1}$ | $w_i$=weight, $\sum w_i = 1$ | units |
| 3 | Exponential Smoothing | $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ | $\alpha$=smoothing constant, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ | units |
| 4 | Mean Absolute Deviation | $MAD = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \vert A_t - F_t \vert$ | forecast error measure | units |
| 5 | Mean Squared Error | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum (A_t - F_t)^2$ | forecast accuracy | units² |
| 6 | Economic Order Quantity | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | D=demand, S=order cost, H=holding cost | units |
| 7 | Total Inventory Cost (EOQ) | $TC = \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$ | annual total cost | ₹/year |
| 8 | Reorder Point | $ROP = d \times L$ | d=daily demand, L=lead time (days) | units |
| 9 | Productivity | $P = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ | output/input | ratio |
| 10 | Multifactor Productivity | $MFP = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{L + K + M}$ | L=labor, K=capital, M=material | ratio |
| 11 | Six Sigma Capability | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\mu$=mean, $\sigma$=std dev | dimensionless |
| 12 | Control Chart Limits (X-bar) | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | grand mean, A2=table constant | units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE SYNTAX NOTE:** In the formulas above, the absolute value bars are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ using LaTeX — this is **not** the markdown pipe character and does **not** break the table.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The EOQ derivation is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU ESE papers.** You must show the full calculus-based derivation.

### Step 1: State the Total Cost Function

The annual total cost of inventory has two components:
- **Ordering Cost** = (Number of orders per year) × (Cost per order)
- **Holding (Carrying) Cost** = (Average inventory) × (Holding cost per unit per year)

**Number of orders per year** = $\dfrac{D}{Q}$, where D = annual demand, Q = order quantity.

**Average inventory** = $\dfrac{Q}{2}$ (assuming inventory depletes linearly from Q to 0).

### Step 2: Construct the Total Cost Equation

$$TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Ordering Cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Holding Cost}}$$

**Textual explanation:** This equation says — every year you spend money on placing orders, and you also spend money storing whatever inventory you have on average. The first term *decreases* as Q grows (fewer orders), while the second term *increases* as Q grows (more stock to hold).

### Step 3: Differentiate TC(Q) with Respect to Q

$$\frac{d\,TC(Q)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}$$

**Textual explanation:** We take the derivative to find the *minimum* of the U-shaped total cost curve. The derivative of $\dfrac{D}{Q}S$ with respect to Q is $-\dfrac{DS}{Q^2}$ (power rule). The derivative of $\dfrac{Q}{2}H$ is $\dfrac{H}{2}$.

### Step 4: Set the Derivative to Zero (Optimality Condition)

At the minimum, slope = 0:

$$-\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

### Step 5: Solve for Q

Move the first term to the other side:

$$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^2}$$

Cross-multiply:

$$H \cdot Q^2 = 2DS$$

Divide by H:

$$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

Take the square root of both sides:

$$\boxed{Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$

### Step 6: Verify with Second Derivative Test

$$\frac{d^2 TC(Q)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \text{(always positive)}$$

**Textual explanation:** The second derivative is *always positive* for positive Q, confirming the critical point is a **MINIMUM** (the curve is convex). This is the **Wilson's Formula** (1923), universally known as **EOQ**.

### Step 7: Derive the Minimum Total Cost

Substitute $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ back into the TC equation:

$$TC^{*} = \frac{D}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} \cdot S + \frac{\sqrt{2DS/H}}{2} \cdot H$$

$$TC^{*} = \frac{DS \cdot \sqrt{H}}{\sqrt{2DS}} + \frac{H}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{DS^2 H}{2DS}} \cdot D + \sqrt{\frac{2DS H^2}{4H}}$$

After simplification (note the elegant symmetry — both terms are equal at EOQ):

$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Example: EOQ for a Kerala Spices Company 🌿

**Problem Statement (KTU Model Question):**
> A spices packaging company in Kochi has an annual demand of **12,000 units** of cardamom boxes. The ordering cost per order is **₹250** and the holding cost per unit per year is **₹20**. Determine:
> (a) Economic Order Quantity
> (b) Number of orders per year
> (c) Time between orders
> (d) Total annual inventory cost

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Given Data:**
- Annual Demand: $D = 12{,}000$ units
- Ordering Cost: $S = ₹250$ per order
- Holding Cost: $H = ₹20$ per unit per year

#### Part (a): Economic Order Quantity

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 250}{20}}$$

Calculate the numerator first:

$$2 \times 12{,}000 \times 250 = 6{,}000{,}000$$

Divide by H = 20:

$$\frac{6{,}000{,}000}{20} = 300{,}000$$

Take the square root:

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{300{,}000} \approx 547.72 \text{ units}$$

**Rounded answer:** $\boxed{Q^{*} \approx 548 \text{ units}}$

#### Part (b): Number of Orders Per Year

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{12{,}000}{548} \approx 21.9$$

**Rounded answer:** $\boxed{N \approx 22 \text{ orders per year}}$

#### Part (c): Time Between Orders (in months)

$$\text{Time} = \frac{12 \text{ months}}{N} = \frac{12}{22} \approx 0.545 \text{ months}$$

**Converted to days:** $0.545 \times 30 \approx 16.4$ days

**Rounded answer:** $\boxed{\text{Order every 16-17 days}}$

#### Part (d): Total Annual Inventory Cost

**Ordering Cost Component:**

$$OC = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} \times S = \frac{12{,}000}{548} \times 250 \approx 21.9 \times 250 = ₹5{,}475$$

**Holding Cost Component:**

$$HC = \frac{Q^{*}}{2} \times H = \frac{548}{2} \times 20 = 274 \times 20 = ₹5{,}480$$

**Total Cost:**

$$TC = OC + HC = 5{,}475 + 5{,}480 \approx ₹10{,}955 \text{ per year}$$

**Verification using the elegant EOQ formula:**

$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 250 \times 20} = \sqrt{120{,}000{,}000} \approx ₹10{,}954.45$$

$$\boxed{TC \approx ₹10{,}955 \text{ per year}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Notice how Ordering Cost ≈ Holding Cost at EOQ. This is a *defining property* of the optimal order quantity — it confirms our calculation is correct. The KTU examiner will often verify your answer using this symmetry.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Forecasting Error Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following worked example demonstrates exponential smoothing — a frequently asked 7-mark problem.**

**Problem:** The quarterly sales of a Kerala handloom cooperative (in ₹ lakhs) for 2022-2023 are given below. Apply **simple exponential smoothing** with $\alpha = 0.3$ and $F_1 = A_1$. Calculate the **MAD** and interpret.

| Quarter | Actual Sales ($A_t$) | Forecast ($F_t$) | Error $\vert A_t - F_t \vert$ |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|------------------------------|
| Q1 2022 | 50 | $F_1 = 50$ | -- |
| Q2 2022 | 54 | $F_2 = 0.3(50) + 0.7(50) = 50$ | $\vert 54-50 \vert = 4$ |
| Q3 2022 | 48 | $F_3 = 0.3(54) + 0.7(50) = 51.2$ | $\vert 48-51.2 \vert = 3.2$ |
| Q4 2022 | 60 | $F_4 = 0.3(48) + 0.7(51.2) = 50.24$ | $\vert 60-50.24 \vert = 9.76$ |
| Q1 2023 | 65 | $F_5 = 0.3(60) + 0.7(50.24) = 53.168$ | $\vert 65-53.168 \vert = 11.832$ |
| Q2 2023 | 62 | $F_6 = 0.3(65) + 0.7(53.168) = 56.7176$ | $\vert 62-56.7176 \vert = 5.2824$ |

**Mean Absolute Deviation:**

$$MAD = \frac{4 + 3.2 + 9.76 + 11.832 + 5.2824}{5} = \frac{34.0744}{5} \approx 6.815$$

$$\boxed{MAD \approx 6.82 \text{ lakhs}}$$

**Interpretation:** On average, our forecasts deviated from actual sales by ₹6.82 lakhs per quarter. The KTU examiner expects you to state whether the model is acceptable (typically MAD < 10% of mean actual = 0.10 × 55.8 = 5.58 is the benchmark). Here, MAD exceeds the benchmark, suggesting the model needs tuning or a trend-adjusted method like **Holt's method** should be used.

## 4.2 SPC Control Chart Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sample[Collect Sample Data]:::startNode
    calcMean[Calculate X-bar and R]:::calcNode
    plot[Plot on Control Chart]:::plotNode

    check[Within Control Limits?\nUCL and LCL]:::checkNode
    outCtrl[OUT OF CONTROL\nInvestigate Assignable Cause]:::alertNode
    inCtrl[IN CONTROL\nContinue Process Monitoring]:::goodNode

    action[Take Corrective Action]:::actionNode
    continue[Proceed to Next Sample]:::nextNode

    sample --> calcMean
    calcMean --> plot
    plot --> check
    check -->|NO| outCtrl
    check -->|YES| inCtrl
    outCtrl --> action
    action --> continue
    inCtrl --> continue

```

### Western Electric Rules for Out-of-Control Detection

A process is declared **out of statistical control** if:
1. **One point** plots outside the **3-sigma** control limits
2. **Two of three consecutive points** plot outside the **2-sigma** limits
3. **Four of five consecutive points** plot outside the **1-sigma** limits
4. **Eight consecutive points** plot on **one side** of the centerline (run rule)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **3-sigma rule** and the **run rule**. Memorize the four conditions above.

### Question 2: Short Application Question
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**

> **Q2.** Distinguish between **common cause variation** and **special cause variation** in Statistical Process Control. Give **one example** of each from a manufacturing context.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Common Cause Variation | Special Cause Variation |
|--------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Nature** | Inherent, random, predictable | Abnormal, assignable, unexpected |
| **Source** | Within the system design | External shock or process change |
| **Magnitude** | Small fluctuations | Large deviations |
| **Action** | Improve the system | Remove the specific cause |
| **Control Chart** | Points within control limits | Point outside 3-sigma limits |

**Manufacturing Examples (1 mark each):**
- **Common cause:** Slight variation in bolt diameter (0.5 mm) due to natural material inconsistency — acceptable within tolerance.
- **Special cause:** Sudden spike in defect rate from 2% to 15% because a CNC machine's cutting tool broke — requires immediate shutdown.

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks Total]**

> **Q. A (a) [7 marks]** A biscuit manufacturing unit in Thrissur requires **15,000 kg** of sugar annually. The ordering cost is **₹400 per order** and the holding cost is **₹15 per kg per year**.
> 
> Calculate:
> 1. Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)
> 2. Optimal number of orders per year
> 3. Minimum total annual inventory cost
> 4. Time between two consecutive orders (in days, assuming 360-day year)

> **Q. A (b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Six Sigma methodology** in production quality. Discuss the **DMAIC framework** with reference to a Kerala-based electronics manufacturing firm.

---

### SOLUTION TO Q.A(a) — Step-by-Step

**Given:**
- $D = 15{,}000$ kg/year
- $S = ₹400$ per order
- $H = ₹15$ per kg/year

#### (1) Economic Order Quantity

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 15{,}000 \times 400}{15}}$$

Compute numerator: $2 \times 15{,}000 \times 400 = 12{,}000{,}000$

Divide by 15: $12{,}000{,}000 \div 15 = 800{,}000$

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{800{,}000} \approx 894.43 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{Q^{*} \approx 894 \text{ kg per order}}$$

#### (2) Optimal Number of Orders

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{15{,}000}{894} \approx 16.78$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 17 \text{ orders per year}}$$

#### (3) Minimum Total Annual Inventory Cost

Using the elegant formula:

$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 15{,}000 \times 400 \times 15} = \sqrt{180{,}000{,}000}$$

$$TC^{*} \approx ₹13{,}416.41$$

$$\boxed{TC^{*} \approx ₹13{,}416 \text{ per year}}$$

**Verification:**
- Ordering cost: $\frac{15{,}000}{894} \times 400 = 16.78 \times 400 = ₹6{,}712$
- Holding cost: $\frac{894}{2} \times 15 = 447 \times 15 = ₹6{,}705$
- Total: $₹6{,}712 + ₹6{,}705 = ₹13{,}417$ ✓ (matches the formula)

#### (4) Time Between Orders

$$\text{Time} = \frac{360 \text{ days}}{17 \text{ orders}} \approx 21.18 \text{ days}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Order approximately every 21 days}}$$

---

### SOLUTION TO Q.A(b) — Six Sigma & DMAIC Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Sigma** is a data-driven quality methodology that aims to reduce process defects to a level of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** by operating at ±6 standard deviations from the process mean. It was pioneered by **Motorola** in 1986 (engineer Bill Smith) and later adopted by **General Electric (Jack Welch)**.

**The DMAIC Framework — 5 Phases:**

| Phase | Full Name | Activities in Kerala Electronics Firm Context |
|-------|-----------|---------------------------------------------|
| **D** | **Define** | Identify the problem: "PCB soldering defects at Keltron factory exceed 5% target" |
| **M** | **Measure** | Collect data: 1,000 PCBs sampled, 62 defects recorded, current $\sigma$ level = 3.2 |
| **A** | **Analyze** | Root cause analysis reveals: (a) Soldering iron temperature drift, (b) Operator training gaps |
| **I** | **Improve** | Implement: (a) Calibrate irons daily, (b) Conduct 8-hour training, (c) Introduce automated optical inspection |
| **C** | **Control** | Set up SPC control charts, monitor daily, train supervisors to maintain improvements |

**Key Six Sigma Tools:**
- **Pareto Charts** (80-20 rule)
- **Fishbone Diagrams (Ishikawa)** — cause-and-effect analysis
- **Control Charts** (X-bar, R, p, c)
- **Process Capability Indices** ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$)
- **Hypothesis Testing** (t-tests, ANOVA)

**Six Sigma Belts Hierarchy (Personnel Roles):**
- **White Belt** → Basic awareness
- **Yellow Belt** → Project team member
- **Green Belt** → Project leader (part-time)
- **Black Belt** → Full-time project leader
- **Master Black Belt** → Mentor and trainer
- **Champion** → Executive sponsor

---

### CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks Total]**

> **Q. B (a) [7 marks]** A textile shop in Kannur recorded the following monthly sales (in ₹ thousands) for the last 6 months: **40, 45, 42, 50, 48, 55**. Apply **Simple Exponential Smoothing** with $\alpha = 0.4$ and initial forecast $F_1 = A_1 = 40$. Forecast the sales for the **7th month** and compute the **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)**.

> **Q. B (b) [7 marks]** Explain the **EOQ model assumptions** and discuss how the model must be **modified** when these assumptions are violated in real industrial practice.

---

### SOLUTION TO Q.B(a) — Exponential Smoothing

**Given:**
- $A_1=40, A_2=45, A_3=42, A_4=50, A_5=48, A_6=55$
- $\alpha = 0.4$, $F_1 = 40$

**Formula:** $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$

#### Step-by-Step Calculation Table

| Month $t$ | Actual $A_t$ | Forecast $F_t$ | Calculation | Absolute Error $\vert A_t - F_t \vert$ |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|-------------|------------------------------------------|
| 1 | 40 | $F_1 = 40$ | Initial | — |
| 2 | 45 | $F_2 = 0.4(40) + 0.6(40) = 40$ | $0.4 \times 40 + 0.6 \times 40$ | $\vert 45-40 \vert = 5$ |
| 3 | 42 | $F_3 = 0.4(45) + 0.6(40) = 42$ | $0.4 \times 45 + 0.6 \times 40$ | $\vert 42-42 \vert = 0$ |
| 4 | 50 | $F_4 = 0.4(42) + 0.6(42) = 42$ | $0.4 \times 42 + 0.6 \times 42$ | $\vert 50-42 \vert = 8$ |
| 5 | 48 | $F_5 = 0.4(50) + 0.6(42) = 45.2$ | $0.4 \times 50 + 0.6 \times 42$ | $\vert 48-45.2 \vert = 2.8$ |
| 6 | 55 | $F_6 = 0.4(48) + 0.6(45.2) = 46.32$ | $0.4 \times 48 + 0.6 \times 45.2$ | $\vert 55-46.32 \vert = 8.68$ |

**Forecast for Month 7:**

$$F_7 = 0.4(55) + 0.6(46.32) = 22 + 27.792 = 49.792$$

$$\boxed{F_7 \approx ₹49.79 \text{ thousand}}$$

**Mean Absolute Deviation:**

$$MAD = \frac{5 + 0 + 8 + 2.8 + 8.68}{5} = \frac{24.48}{5} = 4.896$$

$$\boxed{MAD \approx 4.90 \text{ (₹ thousands)}}$$

**Interpretation:** The forecast for month 7 is approximately **₹49,790** with a typical error of **±₹4,900**. Since the data shows an upward trend and MAD is ~10% of the mean (₹46.67), the model is marginally acceptable; however, **Holt's trend-adjusted method** would improve accuracy.

---

### SOLUTION TO Q.B(b) — EOQ Assumptions and Real-World Modifications

> [!NOTE]
> **The basic EOQ model** (also called the **Wilson-Harris Model**) makes **seven key assumptions**. In real industrial practice, every single one is typically violated, leading to several **modified EOQ models**.

**The Seven Assumptions vs. Real-World Reality:**

| # | Assumption | Real-World Violation | Modified Model |
|---|------------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Constant, known demand** | Demand fluctuates seasonally (e.g., Onam sales spike in Kerala) | **Variable Demand EOQ / Dynamic Lot-sizing** |
| 2 | **Instantaneous replenishment** | Suppliers take days/weeks to deliver | **Production Order Quantity (POQ)** model |
| 3 | **No stockouts allowed** | Firms often tolerate backorders to save holding cost | **EOQ with Planned Shortages (Backordering Model)** |
| 4 | **Constant ordering cost** | Quantity discounts reduce per-unit ordering cost | **Quantity Discount EOQ Model** |
| 5 | **Independent demand** | Components are part of a Bill of Materials (BOM) | **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** |
| 6 | **Single product** | Firms produce hundreds of SKUs | **Joint Replenishment Problem (JRP)** |
| 7 | **No capacity constraints** | Warehouse space, capital are limited | **Constrained EOQ / Lagrangian Optimization** |

**Detailed Discussion of Two Key Modifications:**

#### Modification 1: EOQ with Quantity Discounts

When suppliers offer **all-units discounts** (e.g., 5% off if you order ≥ 1000 units), the total cost becomes:

$$TC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q}S + \frac{Q}{2}H + P(Q) \cdot D$$

where $P(Q)$ is the price per unit, which is a **step function** of Q.

**Algorithm to find optimal Q:**
1. Compute EOQ for the **lowest price** tier
2. If EOQ falls within that tier's quantity range → **optimal**
3. Otherwise, evaluate TC at the **breakpoint** of each higher tier
4. Choose the Q with the **lowest total cost**

#### Modification 2: EOQ with Backorders (Planned Shortages)

If a firm allows customers to wait (backorder) for up to $S_1$ units during stockout periods, the optimal order quantity becomes:

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H + B}{B}}$$

where $B$ = backorder cost per unit per year. The **maximum backorder level** is:

$$S_1^{*} = Q^{*} \cdot \frac{H}{H + B}$$

**Why this matters for KTU students:** When answering exam questions, if the question says *"The firm allows backorders"* or *"There is a 5% quantity discount"*, you **must use the modified formulas**, not the basic EOQ. Examiners deduct marks for using the wrong model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in Production & Operations Analytics questions:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in EOQ (loses 2 marks):** D must be in units/**year** and H in ₹/unit/**year**. If D is monthly, **first convert to annual** by multiplying by 12.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to convert lead time in ROP formula (loses 2 marks):** $ROP = d \times L$ where $d$ is **daily demand** and $L$ is **lead time in days**. If monthly demand is given, divide by 30.
> 
> 3. **Using wrong smoothing constant (loses 1 mark):** In ES formula, students often write $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_{t+1} + ...$ using the *future* actual — it should be $A_t$ (the *previous* actual).
> 
> 4. **Not stating EOQ assumptions (loses 1-2 marks):** When asked to "discuss" EOQ, always list the 7 assumptions. Examiners specifically check this.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Productivity with Efficiency:** Productivity = Output/Input (a ratio). Efficiency = (Standard Output / Actual Input) × 100% (a percentage). They are **NOT** the same.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the second-derivative test in derivation questions:** The 2nd derivative verification ($d^2TC/dQ^2 > 0$) is worth **1 mark** in 7-mark derivation questions. Do not skip it.
> 
> 7. **Confusing $\bar{X}$ chart with $R$ chart constants:** The factor is $A_2$ for X-bar chart, $D_3, D_4$ for R chart, $D_2$ for estimating $\sigma$. Using $A_2$ where $D_4$ is needed costs 1 mark.

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before KTU ESE

**Forecasting Fundamentals:**
- ☐ **Naive forecast:** $F_{t+1} = A_t$ (tomorrow = today)
- ☐ **Simple Moving Average:** Averages last $n$ periods, equal weight
- ☐ **Weighted Moving Average:** Recent periods have higher weights, $\sum w_i = 1$
- ☐ **Exponential Smoothing:** $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ — $\alpha$ close to 1 = reactive, close to 0 = stable
- ☐ **MAD** measures average forecast error in original units
- ☐ **MSE** penalizes large errors more (squares them)
- ☐ **MAPE** = (MAD / Mean Actual) × 100% — scale-independent measure

**Inventory & EOQ Mastery:**
- ☐ EOQ minimizes **sum of ordering and holding costs**
- ☐ $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — Wilson's Formula
- ☐ At optimal Q: **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost** (the "balance point")
- ☐ $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}$ — elegant symmetric formula
- ☐ $ROP = d \times L$ — Reorder Point formula
- ☐ **Quantity discount** model: evaluate TC at each price break
- ☐ **Backorder model:** $Q^*$ increases, average inventory decreases

**Quality Control Essentials:**
- ☐ SPC distinguishes **common cause** (inherent) vs **special cause** (assignable)
- ☐ **Control limits** are at $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm 3\sigma$ (NOT 2σ, that's warning limits)
- ☐ Six Sigma = **3.4 DPMO** at $\pm 6\sigma$ level
- ☐ **DMAIC** = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control
- ☐ Process capability $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is generally acceptable; $\geq 1.67$ is excellent
- ☐ **Western Electric Rules** identify out-of-control conditions

**Productivity Traps to Avoid:**
- ☐ **Single-factor productivity** uses one input (e.g., labor only)
- ☐ **Multifactor productivity (MFP)** uses multiple inputs
- ☐ Productivity *increase* ≠ cost *reduction* (output may also grow)
- ☐ Industry 4.0 in Kerala context = IoT + AI + analytics in production

**High-Frequency KTU Exam Keywords:**
- ☐ "Calculate EOQ" → show full derivation, then numerical substitution
- ☐ "Discuss assumptions" → list all 7, then state what happens when violated
- ☐ "Apply exponential smoothing" → show table with 5+ periods minimum
- ☐ "Interpret control chart" → identify rule violated + probable cause
- ☐ "Compare productivity" → state numerical values + percentage change

**Numerical Benchmarks to Remember:**
- ☐ EOQ constant: $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$
- ☐ 360 days/year for EOQ time-between-orders
- ☐ Six Sigma multiplier: 6 (target defects)
- ☐ DPMO at Six Sigma: 3.4
- ☐ 3-sigma control limits: 99.73% of data within bounds

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip from KTU Board Examiner Perspective:** Always **draw a clearly labeled diagram or table** in your answer sheet — students who visualize their calculations consistently score 10-15% higher than those who only write equations. Use **specific Kerala industrial examples** (Kerala Minerals, Keltron, Cochin Shipyard, HMT) to demonstrate contextual understanding and earn **CO achievement** marks.
